proposals.title: Learning from Imperfect Reasoning: A Mutual Information Framework for Training on Partially Correct Rationales
proposals.motivation: Current methods for training reasoning models, such as process supervision, discard all rationales that lead to incorrect final answers. This is data-inefficient and may discard valuable partial reasoning steps or correct sub-processes. In an era of increasingly complex tasks, learning from imperfect reasoning traces is crucial for sample efficiency and robust generalization.
proposals.hypothesis: Rationales leading to incorrect final answers contain latent, useful reasoning structures that can be identified and leveraged for training, improving model performance and robustness more efficiently than learning from only perfectly correct chains.
proposals.method: We will develop a self-supervised, inductive benchmark to test this hypothesis. Building on Zelikman's prior work on mutual information for principle alignment, we will design an algorithm that treats rationales and final answers as correlated variables. The model will learn to maximize mutual information between embeddings of reasoning steps and a latent 'reasoning quality' signal, not just final answer correctness. This will involve training a critic network to score rationale subsections, creating a new training objective that rewards locally sound reasoning even within globally incorrect chains.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training paradigm that increases data efficiency for reasoning tasks. It will yield a new, publicly released benchmark for evaluating models on partial reasoning capture and a set of principles for constructing training datasets from noisy reasoning traces.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the core assumption in process supervision that a wrong answer implies a worthless rationale. The application of mutual information maximization to extract and weight useful sub-structures within incorrect reasoning chains is a novel technical approach.

proposals.title: PRISM: Pluralistic, Role-Specialized Models for Self-Improvement via Principle-Driven Verification
proposals.motivation: Current self-improvement methods, where a single model generates and scores its own outputs, suffer from confirmation bias and limited critique depth, hindering reliable capability scaling. A modular approach with distinct roles could enable more rigorous, principle-based verification and iterative refinement, mirroring robust human feedback processes but at scale and lower cost.
proposals.hypothesis: A self-improvement loop composed of three separate, specialized models—a Generator, a Verifier, and a Refiner—will produce higher-quality synthetic training data and achieve greater performance gains on reasoning benchmarks than a monolithic self-teaching model, by enforcing a separation of concerns and principled critique.
proposals.method: We will instantiate the loop using three fine-tuned variants of a base pre-trained LLM (e.g., Llama 3). The Generator creates solutions to reasoning tasks (e.g., from MATH or BIG-Bench Hard). The Verifier, trained via self-supervised alignment on mutual information of principles (inspired by Zelikman's prior work), critiques solutions against a structured rubric. The Refiner model then edits the Generator's output based on the Verifier's feedback. This synthetic (problem, refined solution) data will be used to train the next iteration of the Generator, creating a closed loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, reproducible framework for scalable self-improvement that mitigates single-model bias. It will contribute a public benchmark and testbed (extending the PERSONA methodology) for evaluating pluralistic alignment in iterative loops, and demonstrate measurable gains on complex reasoning tasks.
proposals.novelty: While using multiple models for debate or verification exists, applying a fixed, principle-driven triple-specialist architecture to create a fully automated, iterative training loop is novel. It directly tests the benefit of role separation over monolithic self-learning, a core but underexplored inductive principle for AI development.

proposals.title: STaR for Science: Bootstrapping Language Model Reasoning on Complex STEM Problems
proposals.motivation: The STaR (Self-Taught Reasoner) method has shown promise in improving reasoning on relatively simple tasks like arithmetic and commonsense QA. However, scaling such bootstrapping techniques to complex, multi-step domains like scientific reasoning and advanced mathematics remains an open challenge. Success would enable more efficient training of models capable of expert-level reasoning without extensive human supervision, addressing a key bottleneck in AI for science and education.
proposals.hypothesis: The core STaR loop of generating rationales, filtering for correctness, and fine-tuning can be adapted to complex reasoning domains by incorporating structured, principle-based verification and iterative dataset refinement, leading to significant improvements in model performance on challenging STEM benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will extend the STaR framework by integrating it with a principle-driven verification module, inspired by Zelikman's work on principle-based alignment. For tasks from datasets like MATH or scientific QA, the LLM will generate candidate solutions. A separate verifier, trained on inductive principles of logical and mathematical correctness, will filter rationales. The refined, high-quality rationales will be used for iterative model fine-tuning. We will benchmark against standard and chain-of-thought baselines, measuring performance gains on held-out complex problems.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a robust, scalable method for bootstrapping LLM reasoning in complex domains and a new, high-quality generated dataset of verified scientific rationales. It will provide empirical evidence on the limits and extensibility of self-improvement techniques for advanced cognition.
proposals.novelty: While STaR has been applied to simpler tasks, its application to domains requiring rigorous, multi-step deduction with principle-based verification is novel. This bridges Zelikman's expertise in principle-driven methods with reasoning bootstrap techniques, moving beyond preference labels to correctness-driven learning.

proposals.title: Self-Verified STaR: Robust Rationalization via Knowledge Critique and Integration
proposals.motivation: The Self-Taught Reasoner (STaR) and similar methods for bootstrapping reasoning in language models are brittle to noisy or contradictory information from external knowledge retrieval, which can propagate errors into the learned reasoning process. This problem is acute in open-domain QA and complex reasoning tasks where retrieved documents are often irrelevant or conflicting. A principled method to verify and filter the knowledge used in rationales is needed to improve robustness and trustworthiness.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a self-verification module that critiques retrieved knowledge for contradictions and relevance before its use in rationale generation will yield more reliable rationales, leading to improved sample efficiency and final-task performance in STaR-like iterative training.
proposals.method: We will augment the STaR loop with a two-stage process. First, for a given question, a retriever (e.g., DPR) fetches relevant passages. A generator produces a candidate rationale using this context. Second, a dedicated verification module, trained via mutual information maximization (inspired by Zelikman's prior work on self-supervised alignment), analyzes the rationale against the retrieved snippets to produce a self-critique score flagging contradictions, uncertainties, and irrelevancies. Only rationales surpassing a confidence threshold are used for answer generation or added to the training set for the next STaR iteration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a new benchmark for evaluating the robustness of reasoning-with-retrieval systems against adversarial or noisy knowledge sources, and 2) a reproducible framework for self-verified knowledge integration that improves the sample efficiency and final accuracy of iterative reasoning methods.
proposals.novelty: While STaR and retrieval-augmented generation exist, the integration of a trainable, principle-based verification module that actively critiques knowledge *before* its assimilation into the reasoning chain is a novel safeguard against error propagation, moving beyond simple retrieval or post-hoc answer verification.

proposals.title: PRISM: Learning to Filter Rationales via Self-Supervised Auxiliary Losses
proposals.motivation: Chain-of-thought and other rationale-generating methods improve LLM reasoning but produce variable-quality traces, harming efficiency and reliability. Current filtering relies on heuristics or reinforcement learning from final task outcomes, which provides sparse, delayed signals. A method to learn a general, context-aware policy for identifying high-quality reasoning during training is needed to improve the robustness and sample efficiency of reasoning models.
proposals.hypothesis: A parameterized rationale selection module, trained end-to-end using auxiliary self-supervised losses that directly measure rationale quality and utility, will develop a generalizable internal representation of effective reasoning, outperforming rule-based or outcome-only RL filters.
proposals.method: We will train a lightweight, per-token gating network atop a frozen base LLM. This gating network will be trained using auxiliary losses computed on the selected rationale sub-sequence. Losses will target mutual information between the rationale and the final output (inspired by Zelikman's prior work on alignment), rationale consistency via self-agreement checks, and predictive utility for intermediate variables. The gating parameters will be updated to minimize a weighted sum of these auxiliary losses, enabling the model to learn selection criteria without explicit preference labels.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a learned, adaptive filter for rationales that improves reasoning sample efficiency and final task performance. It contributes a novel training paradigm for rationale selection that moves beyond post-hoc scoring, integrating selection directly into the learning objective. The resulting testbed and principles for rationale quality will advance reproducible research in pluralistic alignment.
proposals.novelty: Unlike rule-based filters or RL-from-outcome, our method uses dense, self-supervised auxiliary signals to train the selection mechanism itself, enabling it to learn a dynamic, internal model of rationale quality that generalizes across tasks.

proposals.title: PRISM: Principle-Guided Rationale Induction for Selective Self-Training
proposals.motivation: Chain-of-thought and self-generated rationales are central to improving LLM reasoning, but the quality of generated rationales is highly variable. Current filtering methods rely on simple heuristics or expensive human preference labels, creating a bottleneck for scalable, high-quality self-training. This work addresses the need for an automated, robust method to identify high-fidelity reasoning traces from a noisy pool of model-generated candidates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rationales can be effectively filtered and selected by training a lightweight discriminator to score them based on their adherence to abstract, human-specified reasoning principles, and that using these selected rationales for iterative self-training will yield significant gains in downstream task performance.
proposals.method: We will develop PRISM, a two-stage pipeline. First, we adapt the mutual information alignment framework from Zelikman's prior work to train a rationale discriminator in a self-supervised manner, using contrastive learning to maximize agreement between principle embeddings (e.g., 'step-by-step', 'faithful to premises') and high-scoring rationale embeddings. Second, we integrate this discriminator into an inductive, iterative self-training loop: generate rationales, filter via PRISM, and fine-tune the base model on the high-scoring subset. We will benchmark performance on diverse reasoning tasks (e.g., GSM8K, ARC, ProofWriter).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, label-efficient method for rationale quality assessment based on principle adherence, 2) a reproducible testbed for evaluating rationale selection algorithms, extending the PERSONA framework, and 3) empirical evidence on how high-quality rationale selection impacts self-training scaling laws for reasoning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that filters based on answer correctness or learned reward models, our method explicitly optimizes for the intrinsic reasoning process as defined by composable principles, enabling control over the *style* of induced rationales. This combines self-supervised alignment with iterative dataset curation in a novel way for reasoning tasks.

proposals.title: STaR-EM: A Formal Expectation-Maximization Framework for Bootstrapping Latent Rationales in Chain-of-Thought Learning
proposals.motivation: Self-Taught Reasoner (STaR) and related methods demonstrate that language models can bootstrap reasoning by generating and learning from rationales. However, this bootstrapping process is often heuristic and unstable, lacking a formal probabilistic foundation. A rigorous framework is needed to improve sample efficiency, convergence guarantees, and the quality of learned rationales for complex reasoning tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: Formalizing STaR's iterative bootstrapping as an Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, where rationales are latent variables, will yield a more stable and data-efficient training procedure that provably maximizes a lower bound on the log-likelihood of correct task outputs.
proposals.method: We will frame rationale-augmented training as inference in a latent variable model. The E-step will involve generating or refining rationales for a subset of training examples using the current model, potentially guided by mutual information objectives to ensure rationale utility. The M-step will then update the model parameters by maximizing the likelihood of the (answer, rationale) pairs, treating rationales as observed. We will implement and test variants, including hard EM (selecting best rationale) and soft EM (marginalizing over multiple rationales), benchmarking against standard STaR on mathematical and commonsense reasoning datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, statistically grounded algorithm for rationale bootstrapping, moving beyond ad-hoc implementations. It will deliver a reproducible testbed for studying rationale learning dynamics and could lead to more sample-efficient training of reasoning models. The formal EM lens may also unlock new techniques for controlling rationale properties like faithfulness.
proposals.novelty: While STaR uses a bootstrapping loop, it is not formulated as a proper EM algorithm with a well-defined latent variable model and corresponding lower bound optimization. This formalization and its connection to mutual information-based alignment principles is novel.

proposals.title: Zero-Shot Rationalization: Data-Efficient Chain-of-Thought Learning via Self-Taught Reasoner
proposals.motivation: The Self-Taught Reasoner (STaR) method demonstrates that generating and finetuning on rationales can improve reasoning, but it requires a dataset of seed rationales. In many real-world and specialized domains, such curated rationales are scarce or non-existent. This project addresses the critical challenge of bootstrapping reasoning capabilities in extremely low-data regimes, aligning with the need for efficient, adaptable language models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a modified STaR framework, starting from zero or one in-context rationale examples, can iteratively induce a model's own reasoning principles and generate a high-quality synthetic training set, leading to significant gains in task performance with minimal human annotation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the STaR algorithm for a 'cold-start' setting. Starting with a base language model (e.g., LLaMA-2 or GPT-3), we will use a single, carefully crafted principle-based prompt to generate initial rationales for a small unlabeled task set. We will then implement a rigorous, iterative self-training loop: 1) Generate rationales for a batch of examples, 2) Filter candidates using a consistency-based benchmark (e.g., majority voting or agreement with a verifier), and 3) Finetune the model on the high-confidence (rationale, answer) pairs. The core technical innovation is the design of inductive, principle-guided prompts and filtering mechanisms to bootstrap quality from minimal seeds.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel methodology for data-efficient reasoning induction, a benchmark for evaluating few-shot rationale bootstrapping, and empirical results demonstrating the feasibility of learning complex reasoning from near-zero examples. It contributes directly to research on self-supervised alignment and synthetic dataset generation for reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While STaR requires initial rationales, this proposal pushes the boundary to the extreme low-data regime, exploring whether models can self-discover reasoning principles from almost nothing. It combines Zelikman's expertise in principle-based alignment and benchmark creation with a novel application of iterative self-training.

proposals.title: Iterative Self-Training at Scale: Scaling STaR to Billion-Question Corpora for Emergent Reasoning
proposals.motivation: The Self-Taught Reasoner (STaR) demonstrated that LLMs can bootstrap reasoning via iterative self-generation and refinement. However, prior studies were limited to small, curated datasets (e.g., GSM8K), leaving open whether this bootstrapping process can scale effectively to massive, diverse, and unlabeled question corpora. Scaling STaR could provide a pathway to significantly improve reasoning capabilities without extensive human supervision, addressing a key bottleneck in AI alignment and capability development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that running STaR for many iterations on a massive, unlabeled corpus of questions will lead to emergent improvements in reasoning robustness and generalization, surpassing the gains observed in small-scale, domain-limited applications.
proposals.method: We will implement a large-scale, multi-iteration STaR pipeline. Starting with a base model (e.g., Llama 2 or 3), we will sample millions of diverse questions from a huge, unlabeled corpus (e.g., a filtered subset of the Common Crawl). In each iteration, the model generates reasoning chains and final answers, which are filtered using a correctness criterion (e.g., verifiable answer consistency). The high-quality (question, reasoning, answer) triplets will be used for fine-tuning, creating a new model for the next iteration. We will rigorously benchmark performance on held-out reasoning tasks (e.g., BIG-Bench Hard, MATH) to track generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first empirical study of STaR's scaling laws for reasoning, providing concrete data on performance versus iteration count and dataset size. It will produce a new methodology for large-scale, self-supervised reasoning improvement and release a large, iteratively refined dataset of model-generated reasoning traces.
proposals.novelty: While STaR is known, its systematic application over dozens of iterations on billion-scale, uncurated question sets is unexplored. This bridges Zelikman's interest in principle-driven, self-supervised alignment with the challenge of scaling inductive reasoning methods beyond narrow benchmarks.

proposals.title: Inverse Reasoning: Generating Diverse and Challenging Questions from Rationale Principles
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for evaluating language model reasoning are static and can be saturated, limiting their ability to measure genuine generalization. There is a need for methods to dynamically generate novel, high-quality reasoning tasks to stress-test models and create self-improving curricula. This addresses a core gap in pre-2026 evaluation and training paradigms.
proposals.hypothesis: A model trained to invert the reasoning process—generating valid question-answer pairs from abstract reasoning principles and solution rationales—can produce novel, challenging problems that expose systematic failures in existing models and serve as effective training data.
proposals.method: We will adapt a T5 or encoder-decoder Transformer, training it on datasets like GSM8K and MATH where (question, rationale, answer) triples are available. The model will be trained with a dual objective: (1) a reconstruction loss to generate the original question from its rationale, and (2) a mutual information maximization objective (inspired by Zelikman's prior work on self-supervised alignment) to ensure generated questions adhere to the implicit principles within the rationale. We will benchmark output quality via solvability by a held-out verifier and diversity metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodology for automated benchmark and training dataset creation. It will yield a testbed, 'InverseBench', for evaluating the robustness of reasoning models against systematically generated counterexamples, advancing the field beyond static evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work generates questions from answers or templates, our approach conditions on the full reasoning chain as a principle, making the generation process faithful to complex logical structures. This integrates inverse problem formulation with self-supervised principle alignment.

proposals.title: Iterative Complexity Scaling: A Curriculum for Bootstrapping Deep Reasoning in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current self-improvement methods like STaR (Self-Taught Reasoner) primarily scale by generating more data from a fixed task distribution, which may plateau in reasoning depth. Scaling model parameters alone is computationally expensive and does not guarantee improved complex reasoning. A systematic method to scale task complexity during training is needed to push the frontier of model capabilities without solely relying on parameter count.
proposals.hypothesis: That a curriculum which systematically increases the required reasoning depth and number of steps in tasks during iterative self-training (STaR) will yield language models with superior complex reasoning abilities, outperforming models trained on a static task distribution of equivalent data volume.
proposals.method: We will extend the STaR framework with a dynamic complexity scheduler. Starting from a base dataset (e.g., GSM8K), we will define metrics for reasoning depth (e.g., dependency graph length) and step count. Using a principle-guided generator (inspired by Zelikman's work on principles), we will synthesize new, harder problem variants for each iteration. The model's own successful solutions will be added to the training set, but the generator's parameters will be updated to target higher complexity metrics, creating an automated curriculum. We will benchmark on held-out tiers of MATH and our own generated test suite.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel, automated curriculum learning algorithm for scaling reasoning complexity in LLMs. 2) A new benchmark suite with fine-grained complexity tiers to measure reasoning depth progression. 3) Empirical evidence that scaling task complexity is a viable and efficient path to advanced reasoning, complementary to scaling model size.
proposals.novelty: While curriculum learning is known, applying it to dynamically control the *reasoning structure* of tasks within a self-improvement loop like STaR is novel. This moves beyond simply adding more data to strategically engineer harder reasoning challenges that the model must learn to solve.

proposals.title: PRINCIPLE: A Dual Benchmark for Evaluating Value Articulation and Adherence in Persona-Conditioned Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for AI alignment, such as those derived from PERSONA testbeds, primarily evaluate a model's adherence to implicit values through its behavior or outputs. However, the ability to explicitly articulate the underlying values of a given persona is a critical component of transparency, interpretability, and safe interaction. Pre-2026, there is a lack of systematic evaluation for this explicit reasoning capability, creating a gap in our understanding of whether models can explain the principles they are meant to follow.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model's ability to explicitly articulate a persona's core values is a distinct and measurable capability that correlates with, but is not guaranteed by, its behavioral adherence to those values, and that current models will show a significant performance gap between these two tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct a dual benchmark using a synthetic, principles-first dataset generation pipeline. First, we will define a diverse set of value dimensions and principles, inspired by the pluralistic alignment framework of PERSONA. For each principle set, we will generate (1) a 'Value Explicitness' test, requiring the model to generate clear, abstract statements of the persona's values given a description, and (2) a companion 'Value Adherence' test, comprising situational judgment queries from the PERSONA methodology. We will evaluate models using both automated metrics (e.g., BERTScore against gold principles, entailment checks) and structured human evaluation for explicitness clarity and consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated benchmark for evaluating value explicitness in language models, complementing existing behavioral alignment tests. It will yield a diagnostic tool to separate a model's capacity for principled reasoning from its behavioral compliance, contributing to more nuanced model evaluation and safer, more interpretable persona-based systems.
proposals.novelty: While benchmarks test behavioral alignment, and methods like Zelikman's Self-Supervised Alignment learn principles, no existing benchmark directly and systematically evaluates a model's ability to explicitly *state* the learned or conditioned principles, making this a novel evaluation axis for AI alignment research.

proposals.title: PRISM: Principle-Inducing Self-Improvement from Minimal Seeds
proposals.motivation: Current self-improvement methods for language models rely heavily on high-quality, human-written seed data or principles, creating a bottleneck and potential bias. This dependence limits scalability and the exploration of novel, machine-discovered reasoning strategies. Investigating whether effective self-improvement can be bootstrapped from minimal or random seeds challenges a core assumption in alignment and iterative training research.
proposals.hypothesis: A self-supervised, principle-induction process, guided by mutual information maximization between model outputs and a simple, automatically generated performance signal, can bootstrap meaningful self-improvement from a trivial or randomly generated initial seed, without human-curated starting points.
proposals.method: We will adapt the mutual information framework from 'Self-Supervised Alignment with Mutual Information' to a bootstrapping loop. Starting from a random or single-instruction seed, an LLM will generate candidate solutions for a reasoning benchmark (e.g., GSM8K). A critic model, trained only to predict a coarse performance metric (e.g., final answer correctness), will provide feedback. We will iteratively train the generator to maximize mutual information between its outputs and high critic scores, thereby inducing its own implicit principles for success. The testbed methodology from PERSONA will be used to rigorously evaluate the diversity and quality of the discovered strategies.
proposals.contribution: This work would provide a proof-of-concept for human-minimal self-improvement pipelines, reducing reliance on costly seed data. It would contribute a novel benchmark for evaluating bootstrapping capabilities in LLMs and offer insights into how models can discover latent task principles through interaction with a simple environment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses human principles or preference data as a seed, we explicitly test if the seed is necessary, pushing self-supervised alignment into a bootstrapping regime. The novelty lies in combining mutual information-based principle induction with a randomly initialized task space.

proposals.title: STOP++: Self-Taught Reasoner Optimization for Complex Programming and Reasoning Tasks
proposals.motivation: Self-improvement methods like the Self-Taught Reasoner (STaR) and its successor STOP have shown promise on narrow reasoning tasks (e.g., GSM8K), but their efficacy on more complex, multi-step tasks like competitive programming, theorem proving, and long-horizon planning remains unproven. Scaling self-improvement to these domains is crucial for advancing autonomous AI capabilities without reliance on extensive human-curated data. This work addresses a key gap in the pre-2026 landscape: moving beyond simple arithmetic to tasks requiring structured output, code execution, and formal reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: The STOP method, which uses LLM-generated solutions as preference pairs for alignment via a mutual information objective, can be successfully extended and scaled to induce robust self-improvement on complex programming and reasoning benchmarks, leading to significant performance gains over base models and prior self-improvement techniques.
proposals.method: We will adapt the STOP (Self-Taught Reasoner Optimization) framework, which aligns models using principle-based preferences derived from self-generated solution traces. The core technical extension involves a multi-stage pipeline: 1) generating diverse solution attempts (including code, proofs, and plans) to problems from benchmarks like APPS, TheoremBench, and BabyAI; 2) applying a refined set of inductive, executable principles (e.g., correctness under unit tests, logical coherence, plan consistency) to automatically score and create preference pairs from these traces; 3) training the model using a mutual information loss to maximize the likelihood of high-scoring traces, iterating this process. We will rigorously benchmark against strong baselines, including supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a scalable methodology for self-improvement on complex tasks, a new curated dataset of self-generated solution trajectories with principle-based preferences for programming and reasoning, and a state-of-the-art model for the targeted benchmarks. It will provide empirical evidence for the limits and potential of principle-driven, label-free alignment.
proposals.novelty: While STOP demonstrated self-improvement on simple math, applying and validating its core mutual-information-based alignment on tasks requiring executable code and formal symbolic reasoning is a novel and non-trivial extension. The key innovation lies in designing and testing a suite of automated, executable principles that can reliably generate preference signals from noisy, complex model outputs without human intervention.

proposals.title: STOP-OSS: Self-Training Open-source Policies for Iterative Self-Improvement Without Proprietary Supervision
proposals.motivation: Recent work like STOP (Self-Training Open-source Policies) demonstrates that proprietary LLMs like GPT-4 can generate high-quality synthetic data to iteratively improve smaller models. However, reliance on proprietary models creates reproducibility, cost, and control barriers, limiting widespread research into self-improvement loops. This project investigates whether a fully open-source pipeline can achieve comparable gains, democratizing access to advanced self-improvement techniques.
proposals.hypothesis: A self-improvement pipeline using only open-source models (e.g., Llama 2, Mixtral) for data generation and refinement can produce iterative performance gains on reasoning benchmarks, matching or exceeding the trajectory of proprietary-supervised methods.
proposals.method: We will replicate the core STOP methodology, replacing GPT-4 with a suite of open-source LLMs (e.g., Llama 2 70B, Code Llama) as the generator. The process involves: 1) generating candidate solutions to reasoning tasks (e.g., from MATH or BIG-Bench Hard), 2) using a separate open-source critic model (fine-tuned for verification) to filter solutions based on correctness, and 3) fine-tuning the base model on the filtered synthetic data. We will test inductive principles of self-improvement across multiple cycles, benchmarking each iteration on held-out test sets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a fully reproducible, open-source testbed for studying self-improvement, analogous to the PERSONA testbed for alignment. It will yield a public dataset of iterative model generations and a benchmark for evaluating open-source self-training efficacy, providing critical data on the limits and potential of closed-loop improvement without proprietary components.
proposals.novelty: While STOP uses a proprietary oracle, this project tests if the self-improvement 'principle' is generalizable to a heterogeneous ecosystem of open-source models acting as both generators and critics, a non-trivial challenge given known quality gaps. It uniquely combines Zelikman's focus on principles, generated datasets, and reproducible testbeds.

proposals.title: PRISM: Principle-Driven Iterative Self-Selection for Scalable Hypothesis Refinement
proposals.motivation: Many high-performing reasoning pipelines, like STaR or OPRO, rely on human-in-the-loop selection of intermediate hypotheses or reasoning traces, creating a significant bottleneck for scalability and reproducibility. Automating this selection is crucial for developing truly self-improving language models and for creating large-scale, high-quality generated datasets for complex reasoning tasks without prohibitive human cost.
proposals.hypothesis: A self-supervised, principle-driven scoring function, trained via mutual information maximization, can effectively mimic and potentially surpass human selection for filtering and refining LLM-generated reasoning hypotheses.
proposals.method: We will build on the researcher's prior work on principle alignment without preference labels. The method involves: 1) Generating a diverse set of reasoning hypotheses for a benchmark of reasoning tasks (e.g., GSM8K, ProofWriter). 2) Training a lightweight 'selector' model to score hypotheses by maximizing mutual information between a hypothesis and a distilled set of task-relevant principles (e.g., logical consistency, step correctness), using a contrastive InfoNCE loss. 3) Iteratively applying this selector to filter and generate new training data, creating a self-improving loop. The selector's inductive biases will be explicitly benchmarked against human selections.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, automated pipeline for scalable hypothesis refinement, reducing dependence on human labor. It will yield a public benchmark for automated selection methods and a new, high-quality, principle-aligned dataset of generated reasoning traces for training more robust reasoning models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior reward modeling or simple correctness scoring, this approach leverages unsupervised principle distillation and mutual information to capture the nuanced, multi-dimensional criteria humans use for selection, moving beyond binary correctness towards alignment with procedural and stylistic principles.

proposals.title: PRINCIPLED HYPOTHESIS: Self-Supervised Induction of Diverse and Valid Abstract Principles
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) are powerful tools for generating scientific hypotheses, but they often produce shallow, repetitive, or incorrect abstract principles. This limits their utility as automated partners in discovery, especially in complex domains like social science or ethics where diverse, high-quality conceptual frameworks are needed. Existing methods rely heavily on supervised data or simple prompting, failing to systematically explore the space of valid hypotheses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an inductive, self-supervised training objective based on mutual information and consistency can steer LLMs to generate a broader distribution of abstract hypotheses that are both novel and logically coherent, without requiring human preference labels.
proposals.method: We will adapt the mutual information maximization framework from 'Self-Supervised Alignment with Mutual Information' to the task of hypothesis generation. A generator model will be fine-tuned to produce diverse principle statements, while a separate verifier model—trained on synthetic consistency tests—will score their internal coherence and applicability to example scenarios. We will create a new benchmark, building on the PERSONA testbed methodology, to quantitatively evaluate the diversity (via embedding dispersion) and quality (via logical entailment checks and expert review) of the generated hypotheses against standard prompting baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, label-free fine-tuning method for controlled diversity generation in LLMs, 2) a reproducible benchmark for evaluating hypothesis generation systems, and 3) a dataset of machine-generated, abstract principles with quality annotations. This advances the field towards more capable AI assistants for exploratory reasoning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard prompting or supervised fine-tuning, our approach uses a self-supervised, inductive objective to explicitly optimize for diversity and correctness simultaneously, framing hypothesis generation as a problem of searching a 'principle space' guided by information-theoretic and consistency metrics.

proposals.title: Few-Shot Principle Induction: Efficient Hypothesis Generation for Task-Specific Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current methods for aligning LLMs with human principles or generating task-solving hypotheses often rely on large-scale candidate generation and filtering, which is computationally expensive and slow. In resource-constrained settings (e.g., on-device inference, rapid prototyping, or low-resource domains), there is a critical need for pipelines that can identify high-quality reasoning principles or task hypotheses from only a few candidates.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying a self-supervised, information-theoretic selection criterion—inspired by mutual information alignment—to a minimal candidate set, we can induce effective reasoning principles or task hypotheses that perform competitively with those derived from large-scale generation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the mutual information maximization framework from 'Self-Supervised Alignment with Mutual Information' to operate in a severely constrained candidate space. The pipeline will first use a strong base LLM (e.g., GPT-4 or Claude 3) to generate a very small, diverse set of candidate hypotheses or principles (e.g., 3-10) for a given reasoning task. We will then develop a lightweight, inductive testbed (extending the PERSONA/Media Content Atlas methodology) to evaluate these candidates not on raw performance, but on their mutual information with successful task trajectories in a small demonstration dataset. The candidate maximizing this score will be selected as the induced principle.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method for efficient, few-shot principle induction, directly reducing the computational burden of hypothesis search. It will also contribute a new benchmark for evaluating alignment and reasoning methods under extreme few-shot generation constraints, advancing the field towards more sustainable and agile AI development.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses mutual information for alignment and benchmarks for evaluation, applying this specifically to optimize the *front-end hypothesis generation* phase for extreme data efficiency is novel. The approach moves beyond scaling candidate generation to intelligently scaling down the search process itself.

proposals.title: Benchmark Brittleness: A Systematic Study of LLM Sensitivity to Semantically Invariant Perturbations
proposals.motivation: The field of NLP relies heavily on static benchmarks to measure progress in language model reasoning and task performance. However, recent pre-2026 work has shown that models can be sensitive to superficial formatting changes. This project challenges the foundational assumption that minor, semantically invariant perturbations to benchmark items are negligible, investigating whether they can meaningfully alter comparative model rankings and conclusions about model capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that semantically invariant perturbations (e.g., rephrasing, punctuation changes, or stylistic variations) to benchmark items are not negligible and can systematically alter the relative performance rankings of language models, thereby undermining the reliability of benchmark-based evaluations.
proposals.method: We will develop a perturbation pipeline to automatically generate semantically equivalent variants of items from established reasoning and task-oriented benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, BIG-Bench). Drawing from Zelikman's work on principles and testbeds (PERSONA), we will design an inductive framework to categorize perturbation types (lexical, syntactic, stylistic). We will then conduct a large-scale, controlled evaluation of multiple LLMs (including open- and closed-source) on these variant sets, using statistical tests to measure rank instability and effect sizes, moving beyond simple average performance comparisons.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, empirical framework for assessing benchmark robustness, yielding a public dataset of perturbed benchmark suites. It will contribute a methodology for stress-testing evaluations and offer concrete guidelines for creating more stable and reliable benchmarks in the future.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted sensitivity to prompts, this study systematically isolates and quantifies the impact of semantically *invariant* changes across a broad set of models and tasks, directly testing the core assumption of benchmark stability that underlies most comparative model evaluation.

proposals.title: PRISM: Perturbation-Robust Inductive Scoring for Model Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Current LLM benchmarks are brittle; minor perturbations in phrasing, format, or answer ordering can lead to significant, non-meaningful score fluctuations. This undermines the reliability of benchmark comparisons and our ability to measure true model capabilities. A robust evaluation methodology is needed to distinguish genuine reasoning improvements from superficial sensitivity.
proposals.hypothesis: By inductively learning a scoring function from a diverse set of benchmark perturbations, we can derive a more stable and generalizable evaluation metric that correlates better with human judgments of model capability than raw accuracy on a static test set.
proposals.method: We will create a testbed of semantically equivalent but syntactically varied perturbations for existing reasoning and language understanding benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, BIG-Bench). Drawing from Zelikman's work on inductive principles and mutual information, we will train a lightweight meta-evaluator model to score model outputs. This model will be trained via self-supervised alignment to maximize mutual information between its scores and human robustness judgments across perturbations, while minimizing sensitivity to irrelevant syntactic variations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source framework for perturbation-robust evaluation, moving beyond single-point estimates. It will yield new insights into which model capabilities are genuinely robust and which are artifactually sensitive, offering a more reliable tool for model development and comparison.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work focusing on generating adversarial examples or measuring sensitivity post-hoc, PRISM proposes to inductively learn a robust scoring function as a core evaluation primitive, integrating self-supervised alignment directly into the benchmark scoring process.

proposals.title: Benchmark Robustness Beyond Multiple Choice: A Perturbation Analysis Framework for Generative and Open-Ended Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current LLM benchmark robustness research, like CheckLists and perturbation analysis on MMLU, focuses heavily on multiple-choice formats. However, the field increasingly relies on generative and open-ended benchmarks (e.g., BIG-Bench Hard, HumanEval, instruction-following evaluations) where sensitivity to minor input perturbations is poorly understood. This gap is critical, as it risks overestimating model capabilities and generalizability on complex, real-world reasoning and language generation tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art LLMs exhibit significant and systematic sensitivity to semantically invariant or minor stylistic perturbations on generative and open-ended benchmarks, and that this sensitivity reveals distinct failure modes compared to multiple-choice formats.
proposals.method: We will extend perturbation analysis by designing a principled taxonomy of perturbations for generative tasks, including instruction paraphrasing (using LLM generators), stylistic changes, and contextual framing shifts. Building on Zelikman's work on principles and testbeds (PERSONA), we will develop a scalable, automated pipeline to apply these perturbations to datasets like GSM8K (chain-of-thought), HumanEval (code generation), and AlpacaEval (instruction following). We will then conduct a large-scale empirical study, measuring performance deltas and analyzing error modes using mutual information and statistical measures to quantify robustness, akin to self-supervised alignment analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel, open-source framework for perturbation analysis on generative benchmarks, 2) a dataset of perturbed evaluation samples with robustness scores, and 3) empirical findings that challenge the perceived stability of LLM performance on open-ended tasks. This will provide a more rigorous methodology for benchmark creation and model evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While perturbation analysis is established for classification, its systematic application to generative and free-form reasoning tasks is novel. The approach uniquely combines inductive benchmark design with principles-driven perturbation taxonomies to test the boundaries of model consistency beyond simple answer selection.

proposals.title: PRISM: A Principle-Driven Benchmark for Evaluating Robustness to Semantic and Surface Perturbations
proposals.motivation: Current LLM benchmarks are brittle; minor, semantically neutral perturbations to prompts (e.g., paraphrasing, stylistic changes, or typos) can lead to significant, unprincipled variance in model scores, undermining the reliability of evaluation. This instability makes it difficult to distinguish genuine model capability from evaluation artifacts, a critical problem for both academic research and real-world deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a benchmark constructed from a taxonomy of controlled, semantically-grounded perturbations will reveal systematic failure modes in model robustness and that evaluation stability can be significantly improved by training models to be invariant to these perturbation principles.
proposals.method: We will construct PRISM by applying a suite of perturbation functions to established reasoning and instruction-following datasets (e.g., BBH, MMLU). Perturbations will be categorized by principle (e.g., lexical substitution, syntactic restructuring, informational redundancy, dialect shift) and annotated for semantic equivalence. We will then leverage the researcher's expertise in self-supervised alignment, using a mutual information objective to train models to produce consistent outputs across perturbations of the same underlying task, creating a strong baseline for the benchmark.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a standardized, open-source test suite for quantifying evaluation robustness, moving beyond single-score metrics. It will also demonstrate a novel application of principle-based, self-supervised alignment for improving model invariance, offering a reproducible methodology for building more reliable evaluators.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior perturbation studies focused on adversarial attacks or simple paraphrasing, PRISM is structured around a formal taxonomy of perturbation principles, enabling diagnostic analysis. Furthermore, it integrates benchmark creation with a novel training approach that uses the benchmark's own principles as a self-supervised signal for alignment.

proposals.title: EnsembleBench: Stable LLM Ranking via Aggregated Benchmark Perturbations
proposals.motivation: Current LLM benchmarking is brittle; small changes in prompt phrasing, few-shot examples, or evaluation metrics can dramatically alter model rankings, undermining reliable assessment. This instability makes it difficult to draw robust conclusions about model capabilities, especially for complex reasoning and language tasks. A principled method for producing stable rankings is needed to guide both model development and deployment decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying ensemble methods to aggregate rankings from a diverse set of benchmark perturbations will yield a more stable and reliable ordering of model capabilities than any single benchmark variant. This aggregated ranking will better reflect true underlying model performance.
proposals.method: We will generate a suite of benchmark perturbations for established reasoning and language tasks (e.g., from BIG-Bench, MMLU) by systematically varying prompts, principles (inspired by Zelikman's work on principle alignment), and evaluation metrics. We will then compute model rankings for each variant and apply ensemble ranking techniques, such as Borda count, rank aggregation via Markov chains, and supervised meta-learning, to produce a final consolidated ranking. The stability of the ensemble ranking will be tested against held-out perturbations and compared to baseline single-variant rankings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, open-source framework for robust LLM evaluation that reduces sensitivity to benchmark design choices. It will yield a new set of stable, ensemble-derived rankings for contemporary models, offering the community a more reliable performance metric. The methodology also serves as a reproducible testbed for future benchmark development, advancing the field of evaluation science.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble methods are classic in ML, their systematic application to consolidate rankings across *benchmark design perturbations*—rather than model predictions—is novel for LLM evaluation. This approach directly addresses the core instability problem in a way that goes beyond simply creating a new, static benchmark.

proposals.title: Metrological Benchmarking: A Measurement Error Framework for Robust LLM Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Current LLM benchmarking treats reported scores as deterministic, ignoring inherent noise from sampling, prompt variation, and ambiguous rubrics. This leads to unreliable comparisons, where small score differences are overinterpreted, and hinders reproducible progress in model development. A principled statistical framework is needed to quantify and account for this evaluation uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: By modeling benchmark scores with explicit error distributions from metrology, we can produce more robust, uncertainty-aware performance estimates and rankings, revealing that many claimed performance differences between models are not statistically significant.
proposals.method: We will adapt classical measurement error models (e.g., additive, multiplicative, or heteroscedastic error) to fit empirical score distributions from repeated evaluations on diverse benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, BIG-Bench). Using a Bayesian hierarchical framework, we will infer a model's 'true' latent score and its associated credible interval. The approach will be validated via controlled simulation and by re-analyzing scores from models like Llama 2, GPT-4, and open-source alternatives, testing for rank stability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal statistical toolkit for benchmarking that quantifies evaluation uncertainty, leading to more reliable model comparisons. It will also yield diagnostic metrics to identify 'noisy' benchmark tasks, guiding future evaluation design toward more reliable measurement.
proposals.novelty: While statistical analysis of benchmarks exists, applying formal measurement error models from metrology to decompose and model LLM evaluation noise is novel. This moves beyond simple confidence intervals to a generative model of the entire measurement process.

proposals.title: DUAL: Domain-Invariant Alignment for Robust Benchmarking via Principle-Based Adversarial Training
proposals.motivation: Current LLM benchmarks are riddled with superficial quirks and biases, from stylistic variations to irrelevant contextual details, which models can exploit for high scores without genuine reasoning. This undermines benchmark validity and leads to overestimation of model capabilities. A method to make models robust to these perturbations, rather than perpetually fixing the benchmarks, is a critical unsolved problem in reliable evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: Language models can be trained to be invariant to irrelevant benchmark perturbations by aligning them with abstract task principles, and this principle-aligned robustness will generalize to unseen benchmark variants and real-world tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the principle-based alignment method from 'Self-Supervised Alignment with Mutual Information' to create DUAL. First, we will use multimodal LLMs (inspired by the Media Content Atlas pipeline) to automatically generate 'perturbation families' for existing benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, BIG-Bench), altering irrelevant surface features. Then, we will train models using a contrastive objective that maximizes mutual information between the model's internal representations of a task's core principle and its outputs across all perturbations, while minimizing information related to the perturbation style. This creates a self-supervised, adversarial training loop where the model learns a perturbation-invariant mapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training framework for building robust, principle-grounded models, reducing benchmark overfitting. It will also yield a new reproducible testbed (extending PERSONA) for evaluating model robustness, shifting the field's focus from benchmark engineering to model invariance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work identifies benchmark flaws or uses adversarial data augmentation, DUAL uniquely combines self-supervised principle alignment with systematic, model-generated perturbation families to learn invariance directly, a non-obvious inductive bias for generalization.

proposals.title: PRISM: A Hybrid Topological Metric for Language Model Evaluation via Supervised and Unsupervised Signal Fusion
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of language models, especially on reasoning tasks, relies heavily on supervised benchmarks, which are expensive to create and can overfit to specific datasets. Conversely, purely unsupervised metrics, such as those based on mutual information or topological data analysis (TDA), offer generalizability but may lack task-specific discriminative power. A robust, hybrid metric is needed to combine the precision of supervised signals with the broad coverage of unsupervised structural analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid metric, which fuses supervised task performance signals with unsupervised topological features of model-generated text or latent representations, will yield a more robust, generalizable, and interpretable measure of model capability than either approach alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline inspired by the Media Content Atlas and PERSONA testbed to generate a diverse corpus of model outputs for reasoning tasks. Unsupervised signals will be extracted using TDA (e.g., persistent homology) on sentence embeddings and mutual information estimates between principles and outputs, as in Zelikman's prior work on self-supervised alignment. Supervised signals will be derived from benchmark performance scores. We will then train a lightweight, inductive model (e.g., a graph neural network or meta-learner) to learn an optimal fusion of these signals, validated for its correlation with held-out task performance and its ability to detect model failures.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, publicly released metric (PRISM) for model evaluation, 2) a reproducible framework for constructing hybrid evaluation metrics, and 3) new insights into the relationship between the topological structure of generated text and downstream task performance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses either supervised benchmarks or unsupervised principles, the explicit, learned fusion of topological TDA features with supervised performance scores to create a unified evaluation metric is a novel and non-obvious synthesis.

proposals.title: Topological Stability in the Few-Shot Regime: Evaluating Generative Model Metrics with Limited Samples
proposals.motivation: Topological metrics, such as those based on persistent homology, have shown promise for evaluating generative models by quantifying the geometric fidelity of generated data manifolds. However, their application assumes access to many samples, which is computationally expensive and often impractical for large models or iterative training. It is unknown if these metrics remain reliable and stable when computed from only a few generated samples, a critical gap for efficient model development and benchmarking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that with careful inductive bias and statistical correction, a topological metric's rank-ordering of generative models can remain robust in few-shot regimes, but its absolute value will exhibit predictable variance that can be modeled and calibrated.
proposals.method: We will adapt the topological data analysis pipeline from works like the 'Media Content Atlas' to a controlled few-shot setting. Using a benchmark suite of generative models (e.g., VAEs, GANs, diffusion models) on standard image and text datasets, we will systematically subsample the generated distributions. We will test the reliability of the metric by measuring its correlation with full-data evaluations and its consistency across random few-shot draws, employing principles from self-supervised learning and mutual information estimation to derive a variance-corrected, few-shot stable metric.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for applying powerful topological evaluation in low-data scenarios, significantly reducing the computational cost of model assessment. It will yield a new, reproducible testbed for few-shot metric analysis and offer concrete guidelines for researchers on the sample sizes required for reliable evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While few-shot learning is well-studied for model training, the systematic analysis of evaluation metrics—particularly complex topological ones—under data scarcity is novel. This bridges the gap between advanced geometric evaluation and practical constraints in model development.

proposals.title: Generative Context Engines: A Testbed for Evaluating Evaluation Metrics
proposals.motivation: Current LLM evaluation is a closed-loop system: models are trained and benchmarked on similar datasets, creating a circular dependency where metrics may not capture true contextual reasoning. This risks overfitting to static benchmarks and fails to assess a model's ability to dynamically adapt to nuanced, real-world context. A principled test of evaluation metrics requires a generative source of complex, controllable context that is independent of the training data of the models being evaluated.
proposals.hypothesis: By first building a controllable, context-aware generation model (a 'Generative Context Engine'), we can produce a novel, structured testbed of reasoning tasks; this will reveal significant gaps in the sufficiency of current static evaluation metrics for measuring contextual adaptation.
proposals.method: We will develop a Generative Context Engine by fine-tuning a large language model (e.g., Llama 2 or Mistral) using a self-supervised, principle-based alignment method inspired by Zelikman's prior work on mutual information. This model will be trained to generate multi-step reasoning tasks (e.g., contextual Q&A, principle-based story generation) where the core logical structure and required contextual dependencies are programmatically specified and controlled via a synthetic data pipeline. The output will be a benchmark dataset, GENCONTEXT, where each instance includes the generative 'context parameters,' the resulting complex task, and a verified solution.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel methodology for generating evaluative tasks with known structure, creating a 'stress test' for evaluation metrics; 2) the GENCONTEXT dataset and testbed, enabling reproducible analysis of whether current metrics (e.g., accuracy, BLEU, LLM-as-judge) correlate with true contextual fidelity; 3) a framework for inverting the benchmark development pipeline from static curation to dynamic generation.
proposals.novelty: Instead of creating another static benchmark, we propose building a generative model whose explicit purpose is to create a dynamic, principled testbed for evaluation itself. This directly inverts the standard focus, using generation to probe the limits of evaluation, a approach not yet systematized in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: PRISM: Principle-Driven Refinement of Image-Semantic Metrics via Contextual CLIPScore
proposals.motivation: Standard image-text alignment metrics like CLIPScore are brittle, failing to account for nuanced contextual constraints (e.g., 'a small dog' vs. 'a large dog'). This limits their utility for evaluating and training multimodal models on complex, principle-driven tasks. A robust, context-aware metric is needed to better assess semantic fidelity in generative and retrieval systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By augmenting CLIPScore with a trainable, context-modeling module that encodes task-specific principles, we can create a metric that correlates more strongly with human judgment on nuanced alignment tasks than baseline scores.
proposals.method: We will develop a Contextual Adapter Network that ingests a textual principle (e.g., 'emphasize size') alongside the image and caption. This adapter, built on a lightweight transformer, will generate context-aware embeddings for the image and text. We will train it using a self-supervised, mutual information objective inspired by Zelikman's prior work, maximizing alignment between principle-conditional embeddings. Validation will involve a new, inductively constructed benchmark of image-caption pairs with annotated contextual violations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, open-source metric for nuanced multimodal evaluation and a principled benchmark dataset. It will provide a testbed for studying how explicit principles can be integrated into alignment models, advancing beyond simple preference learning.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior CLIPScore extensions, our method explicitly models free-form contextual principles as trainable inputs. It leverages self-supervised alignment on mutual information, avoiding the need for costly human preference labels during metric training.

proposals.title: Compositional Concept-Vector Rationales: A Testbed for Auditable, Latent-Space Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current methods for generating model rationales present a trade-off: natural language is interpretable but verbose and inefficient for internal computation, while latent vectors are efficient but opaque, hindering trust and auditability. This is a critical bottleneck for deploying reliable AI in high-stakes reasoning tasks. A method that combines the efficiency of latent reasoning with the transparency of symbolic primitives is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: By constructing rationales as compositions of discrete, interpretable concept tokens and their latent relational vectors, enforced via a vector-quantized bottleneck and a deterministic decoder, we can achieve efficient latent-space reasoning with guaranteed decodability to human-auditable symbolic phrases.
proposals.method: We will design a model with a vector-quantized variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE) to learn a discrete codebook of concept vectors from data. A reasoning module will perform sparse manipulations of these vectors. Crucially, a lightweight, fixed transformer decoder will be trained to deterministically map any valid composition of concept vectors back to a natural language phrase describing the concept and its relational modifiers, ensuring guaranteed decodability. We will benchmark this system on synthetic and real-world reasoning tasks from datasets like ProofWriter or PrOntoQA.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architecture for auditable step-by-step reasoning and a reproducible testbed, akin to PERSONA, for evaluating the trade-offs between efficiency and interpretability. It will provide a new inductive benchmark for testing the compositional generalization and principle-following capabilities of AI systems, extending research on self-supervised alignment.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on latent rationales or discrete bottlenecks, this approach guarantees that every internal computational state is *provably decodable* to a human-interpretable phrase via a deterministic, lightweight decoder, creating a true hybrid of symbolic and sub-symbolic AI.

proposals.title: Auditing Faithfulness: A Self-Correcting Loop for Rationale Generation via Adversarial Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often generate rationales that are unfaithful to their internal reasoning or contain subtle biases, undermining trust and performance in reasoning tasks. Current self-correction methods, which use the model's own output as a training signal, can compound these errors. There is a critical need for an external, reliable standard to evaluate and improve rationale faithfulness, a problem highlighted by pre-2026 work on chain-of-thought faithfulness and the limitations of self-consistency checks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a separately trained 'Rationale Auditor' model, specialized in detecting unfaithfulness and bias, can provide a robust reward signal for reinforcement learning (RL) that guides a primary model to generate more faithful and less biased rationales, creating an effective self-correcting loop.
proposals.method: We will first construct a benchmark dataset of rationales with labeled faithfulness and bias flaws, building on principles from the PERSONA testbed. We will then train a specialized 'Rationale Auditor' model using contrastive or mutual information objectives, akin to Self-Supervised Alignment, to distinguish faithful from unfaithful rationales. This auditor will score rationales generated by a primary model (e.g., a T5 or LLaMA variant), and these scores will be converted into rewards for RL fine-tuning (e.g., PPO or REINFORCE) of the primary model, creating an iterative improvement loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel adversarial training framework for improving rationale faithfulness, 2) a new, reproducible benchmark and auditor model for evaluating rationale quality, and 3) empirical evidence on the limits of self-correction and the efficacy of external auditing for alignment tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses self-consistency or human feedback, our approach is novel in using a separately trained adversarial model as an automated, high-precision source of RL reward specifically targeting rationale unfaithfulness, creating a closed-loop self-correction system grounded in an external standard.

proposals.title: Rationale Biopsy: Diagnosing and Treating Systemic Flaws in LLMs via Generated Reasoning Traces
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) generate rationales via chain-of-thought, but these are primarily used to boost answer accuracy. However, these rationales may contain unfaithful reasoning, social biases, or logical inconsistencies that are not reflected in the final token, posing risks for trustworthy deployment. Current benchmarks and preference alignment methods often assess final outputs, lacking tools to systematically diagnose and correct flaws in the internal reasoning process itself.
proposals.hypothesis: Generated rationales can serve as an effective diagnostic probe; systematic analysis of these reasoning traces can identify specific, systemic flaws, and these diagnostics can guide targeted interventions that improve the model's fundamental generation process more effectively than optimizing for final-answer accuracy alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a pipeline inspired by Zelikman's work on principles and testbeds. First, we generate rationales from a base LLM on a curated benchmark of reasoning tasks. Second, we train lightweight, interpretable diagnostic classifiers (e.g., linear probes or small transformers) on these rationales to detect specific flaws like unfaithfulness or bias, establishing a 'Rationale Atlas'. Third, using mutual information objectives akin to self-supervised alignment, we will use these diagnostic signals as a training objective for a controlled intervention, such as constrained decoding with learned constraints or targeted adversarial fine-tuning, to correct the identified flaw in the model's generative process.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel methodology for using rationales as diagnostic probes, 2) a reproducible testbed for evaluating reasoning flaws, and 3) a demonstration of how diagnostic signals can drive interventions that improve attributes like faithfulness, independent of task accuracy. It advances the field beyond using rationales solely for performance.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is decoupling the rationale's role from direct answer prediction, treating it instead as a biopsy for diagnosis. This shifts the focus from optimizing the rationale for a correct answer to analyzing it as a trace of the model's internal process, enabling a new class of targeted, principle-driven corrections.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning to Generate Utility-Maximizing Rationales via Verifier Feedback
proposals.motivation: Large language models often generate rationales that are plausible but unhelpful for task solution, a form of 'reasoning misalignment.' Existing methods, like chain-of-thought prompting or supervised fine-tuning on human rationales, optimize for surface features or human preference, not for the downstream utility of the reasoning. This creates a gap where models learn to imitate reasoning patterns without ensuring they are genuinely instrumental for solving the task.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a rationale generator via meta-gradients from a verifier model—which must solve the task using only the rationale—will produce rationales whose quality is directly defined by their task utility, leading to more faithful and effective reasoning.
proposals.method: We will implement a two-model system: a primary generator (G) and an auxiliary verifier (V). For a given input, G produces a rationale. V is trained to predict the final answer using only this rationale, not the original input. The core innovation is training G via a meta-learning objective: we compute the gradient of V's task loss with respect to G's output rationale. This gradient is used to update G, encouraging rationales that maximally improve V's performance. We will test this using transformer-based architectures on benchmark reasoning datasets (e.g., GSM8K, ARC).
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel training paradigm that directly optimizes rationale utility rather than human-likeness. It will provide a new, reproducible testbed for evaluating faithful reasoning in language models. The resulting method and analysis will offer principles for moving beyond preference-based alignment for complex cognitive tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses verifiers for output scoring or trains generators via reinforcement learning with a verifier reward, our approach uniquely uses the verifier's task-loss gradient as a direct, non-proxy training signal for the generator through meta-learning, creating a closed-loop where rationale quality is defined by its sufficiency for task solution.

proposals.title: Self-Consistent Reasoning: Training Language Models with Bidirectional Rationale-Text Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly generate reasoning rationales (e.g., chain-of-thought) to improve task performance. However, pre-2026 research highlights a critical gap: these rationales can be unfaithful or disconnected from the final output, acting as post-hoc justifications rather than genuine reasoning traces. This undermines trust, interpretability, and the utility of rationales for model improvement, creating a need for methods that ensure rationale faithfulness without costly human supervision.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly training a primary model to generate task outputs from rationales and an auxiliary inverse model to reconstruct rationales from outputs will create a consistency cycle. This cycle will pressure the primary model to generate rationales that are semantically embedded in and recoverable from its final text, thereby improving their intrinsic quality and faithfulness.
proposals.method: We will train a primary language model with a composite loss: a standard causal LM loss on the final answer text (conditioned on a rationale) and an auxiliary consistency loss. The auxiliary loss trains a separate, lightweight inverse model (e.g., a small transformer) to accurately reconstruct the primary model's generated rationale from the final answer text. The gradient from this inverse model's reconstruction error will be backpropagated through the generated text to the primary model's rationale generation module, encouraging it to produce rationales that make the final answer semantically 'reconstructible'. We will benchmark on diverse reasoning datasets (e.g., GSM8K, StrategyQA) and test for improvements in both task performance and rationale faithfulness metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, self-supervised training objective for improving rationale faithfulness, directly addressing a key challenge in interpretable AI. It will provide a new benchmark and testbed for evaluating rationale-text consistency, extending the researcher's prior work on pluralistic alignment and principle-based learning. The method offers a pathway to higher-quality reasoning traces without preference labels, advancing the field of self-supervised alignment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses separate reward models or preference learning for rationale quality, our approach is novel in establishing a direct, differentiable cycle of consistency between rationale and text within the training loop itself. It frames rationale faithfulness as a bidirectional reconstruction problem, a concept not yet explored in pre-2026 chain-of-thought or self-alignment literature.

proposals.title: PRISM: Principle-Driven Rationale Improvement with Self-Supervised Mutual Information
proposals.motivation: Chain-of-thought and rationale generation have become standard for improving LLM reasoning, but training primarily via standard language modeling loss optimizes for fluency over utility. This can lead to plausible but unhelpful or misaligned rationales that fail to guide correct answers. A principled method to directly optimize rationale quality, without costly human preference labels, is a critical open problem in making reasoning more reliable and interpretable.
proposals.hypothesis: Introducing auxiliary self-supervised losses based on mutual information and principle alignment can directly improve the internal consistency and task-relevance of generated rationales, leading to better downstream task performance than standard language modeling alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the SSA-MI (Self-Supervised Alignment with Mutual Information) framework to the rationale generation setting. For a given task, we will define a set of high-level reasoning principles (e.g., 'avoid contradictions', 'maintain relevance to the query'). An auxiliary loss will maximize the mutual information between the generated rationale and a latent representation of these principles. A second contrastive loss will distinguish between principle-consistent and -inconsistent rationale perturbations, using a synthetic testbed like PERSONA for controlled evaluation. These losses will be combined with the standard NLL loss during fine-tuning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, label-efficient training paradigm for rationale generation that directly optimizes for principled reasoning. It will yield a new benchmark for evaluating rationale utility beyond surface fluency, and models that produce more reliable and interpretable chains of thought.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses principles for final-answer alignment, applying self-supervised mutual information maximization directly to the rationale generation process to shape intermediate reasoning is novel. This moves beyond post-hoc filtering or preference tuning of rationales.

proposals.title: Scaling Implicit Chain-of-Thought: Applying and Evaluating Quiet-STaR on Multi-Billion Parameter Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often lack robust, implicit reasoning capabilities, performing reasoning only when explicitly prompted. The Quiet-STaR method, which trains models to generate internal 'thoughts' for each token during standard language modeling, promises to instill more systematic reasoning but has only been demonstrated on models up to 7B parameters. Scaling this self-supervised, principle-driven approach to state-of-the-art models (e.g., 70B+ parameters) is crucial to test if it can significantly enhance complex reasoning and generalization without explicit supervision or preference data.
