proposals.hypothesis: Scaling the Quiet-STaR training framework to significantly larger base models (e.g., from Llama 2 7B to 70B or CodeLlama 34B) will yield greater improvements in zero-shot reasoning and instruction-following capabilities than those observed at smaller scales, and will do so more efficiently than standard supervised fine-tuning on reasoning data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Quiet-STaR algorithm, which interleaves generated 'reasoning' tokens with standard text tokens during training, for efficient large-scale training. Using the Llama 2 family (7B, 13B, 70B) as base models, we will implement distributed training to manage the memory overhead of parallel thought generation. We will train on a large, diverse corpus of web text (e.g., a refined version of the RedPajama dataset) and benchmark performance on a suite of inductive reasoning and principle-following tasks (e.g., BBH, GSM8K, and a novel testbed inspired by PERSONA) to measure improvements in implicit reasoning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical evaluation of scaled self-supervised reasoning training, establishing whether the benefits of Quiet-STaR compound with model size. It will produce a set of large, publicly released models with enhanced implicit reasoning abilities and a reproducible benchmark for assessing reasoning learned through generative pre-training, contributing directly to the field's understanding of how to build more capable and general AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While scaling models is standard, applying and rigorously testing the Quiet-STaR algorithm—a specific, non-obvious method for learning implicit reasoning—at the scale of frontier open-source LLMs (70B+ parameters) is unexplored territory. The project uniquely combines Zelikman's focus on principle-driven, self-supervised alignment with the challenge of scaling a computationally intensive, novel training paradigm.

proposals.title: Aligning Maps of Meaning: Cross-Modal Attention as a Unified Coordinate System for Multimodal Rationales
proposals.motivation: Multimodal models achieve strong performance but remain black boxes, lacking interpretable, fine-grained rationales for their decisions. This opacity hinders trust, debugging, and scientific understanding, especially for complex tasks requiring reasoning across text, image, and audio. Existing methods for unimodal rationales, like tokenwise saliency, do not naturally extend to explaining cross-modal interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly aligning token, patch, and audio-frame representations into a shared 'reasoning space' via learned cross-attention layers, the resulting attention maps will provide faithful, human-interpretable rationales for multimodal model predictions at a granular, token/patch level.
proposals.method: We will design a novel encoder architecture with modality-specific encoders (e.g., ViT, audio spectrogram transformer, text encoder) whose outputs are projected into a common dimensionality. A stack of cross-modal attention layers will facilitate interaction, inspired by GIS coordinate registration. The core output is a dense, explainable attention tensor between all input elements across modalities. We will train this model using a self-supervised, inductive objective based on maximizing mutual information between aligned multimodal views of the same concept, extending principles from Zelikman's prior work on alignment without preference labels, and evaluate rationale quality on a new, curated benchmark of multimodal reasoning tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel neural architecture for generating unified, tokenwise multimodal rationales, 2) a self-supervised training framework for cross-modal alignment grounded in mutual information, and 3) a new reproducible benchmark for evaluating the fidelity and utility of multimodal explanations, advancing the field of interpretable multimodal AI.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit GIS-inspired framing of cross-modal attention as a coordinate alignment problem to produce structured rationales, moving beyond post-hoc explanation methods or unimodal techniques. This integrates self-supervised alignment principles directly into a rationale-generating architecture.

proposals.title: MIRAGE: Multimodal Interpretable Rationale Generation for Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
proposals.motivation: While tokenwise rationale generation has advanced interpretable reasoning in language models, modern AI systems are increasingly multimodal. Existing multimodal LLMs often act as 'black boxes,' providing answers without transparent reasoning steps for visual or audio inputs. This lack of interpretable, step-by-step reasoning hinders debugging, trust, and the ability to learn principled reasoning from multimodal data, a core challenge in pre-2026 AI alignment and robustness research.
proposals.hypothesis: By extending self-supervised, tokenwise rationale generation frameworks to multimodal inputs, we can train models to produce human-interpretable reasoning chains for image- and audio-based tasks, improving both performance on complex multimodal reasoning benchmarks and model alignment with underlying task principles.
proposals.method: We will extend the mutual information-based self-supervised alignment framework from Zelikman's prior work to multimodal encoders. Specifically, we will develop a model that takes interleaved image, audio, and text inputs and generates a token-by-token rationale in a shared latent space before producing a final answer. We will create a new benchmark, building on the PERSONA testbed methodology, containing inductive reasoning tasks requiring cross-modal inference (e.g., visual scene understanding with audio context). Training will use a contrastive objective to maximize mutual information between generated rationales and the correct application of task principles, without need for preference labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel method for self-supervised, interpretable reasoning with multimodal data, 2) a new reproducible benchmark for evaluating multimodal chain-of-thought, and 3) empirical evidence on how rationale generation improves principle adherence in multimodal tasks, advancing the field of pluralistic alignment.
proposals.novelty: This approach is non-obvious as it moves beyond applying CoT as a simple prompting technique, instead integrating rationale generation directly into the multimodal training loop via self-supervised alignment, creating an inherently interpretable model rather than a post-hoc explanation.

proposals.title: Constrained Rationale Alignment: RL for Faithful and Helpful Chain-of-Thought
proposals.motivation: Large language models often generate plausible but unfaithful rationales that do not reflect their true reasoning, a problem known as 'hallucination of thought.' This undermines trust and reliability in high-stakes applications like scientific analysis or medical QA. Existing alignment methods, like RLHF, optimize for human preference but lack explicit mechanisms to enforce internal consistency between the rationale and the model's decision process.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a reinforcement learning policy, trained with a composite reward function and a faithfulness constraint derived from causal influence and consistency under input perturbations, can generate rationales that are both highly helpful and verifiably aligned with the model's internal computations.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a language model using a constrained RL algorithm, such as PPO-Lagrangian. The reward function will combine a helpfulness score (from a reward model or a benchmark metric) with a faithfulness penalty. Faithfulness will be measured via two pre-2026 techniques: (1) the causal influence of the rationale on the final output using input ablation, and (2) the consistency of the rationale's meaning under semantic-preserving input paraphrases. This penalty will be treated as a hard constraint during optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel training framework for faithful reasoning that directly operationalizes and enforces internal alignment. It will produce a new benchmark dataset of tasks with annotated rationale faithfulness, and a model whose rationales are more robust and trustworthy for user interpretation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses post-hoc faithfulness tests, we integrate faithfulness as a trainable, causal constraint within the RL loop. This moves beyond inductive principles to a direct, testable optimization for truthful process representations.

proposals.title: RationaleRL: Optimizing Chain-of-Thought Utility via Reinforcement Learning from Principle-Based Feedback
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) often generate step-by-step rationales (Chain-of-Thought) to improve task performance, but the utility of these rationales for a human reader—such as their clarity, pedagogical value, and efficiency—is not explicitly optimized during standard training. Current methods like supervised fine-tuning on human-written rationales or preference alignment (e.g., DPO) do not directly target a dense, learned reward function for rationale quality, limiting their ability to produce consistently helpful reasoning traces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying reinforcement learning (RL) with a reward model trained to score rationale helpfulness will produce LLMs that generate rationales with higher utility for end-users, as measured by both downstream task accuracy and human evaluation of explanation quality.
proposals.method: We will build on principles from Zelikman's work on principle-based alignment without preference labels. First, we will use a pre-trained LLM to generate diverse rationales for a benchmark of reasoning tasks (e.g., GSM8K, MATH). Second, we will train a reward model using mutual information maximization (inspired by Self-Supervised Alignment) to score rationales based on their adherence to helpfulness principles (e.g., coherence, conciseness, correctness), avoiding the need for human preference labels. Third, we will fine-tune the base LLM using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to maximize this learned reward, conditioning the policy on both the task and target helpfulness principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel RL framework for directly optimizing the utility of generated rationales, 2) a reproducible testbed (extending PERSONA concepts) for evaluating rationale quality across multiple helpfulness dimensions, and 3) empirical evidence on how RL from principle-based rewards affects both reasoning performance and explanation transparency.
proposals.novelty: While RLHF has been used for aligning final answers, its application to optimize the intermediate reasoning process for human-centric utility is novel. The key innovation is combining a self-supervised reward model based on mutual information with RL to train a rationale generation policy, moving beyond supervised distillation or outcome-only alignment.

proposals.title: Quiet-Distill: Inducing Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in Small Language Models via Self-Supervised Principle Alignment
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit impressive reasoning via internal 'chain-of-thought' (CoT), but deploying such models is resource-intensive. Methods like Quiet-STaR show LLMs can learn to reason by self-generating and learning from internal rationales, yet this capability remains confined to large-scale models. Enabling efficient, smaller student models to perform reliable CoT reasoning would significantly broaden the accessibility and application of advanced reasoning systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the self-supervised reasoning principles underpinning Quiet-STaR can be distilled into small models (e.g., <1B parameters) via a tailored alignment process, inducing robust CoT capabilities without reliance on large-scale preference data or extensive human annotation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Quiet-STaR framework, where a model generates and learns from internal rationales to maximize mutual information between thoughts and future tokens, for distillation. A large, frozen teacher model (e.g., GPT-4) will generate a synthetic, principle-annotated dataset of reasoning traces across diverse tasks. We will then train a small student model using a modified version of the Mutual Information-based alignment objective from Zelikman's prior work, enforcing consistency between the student's latent reasoning steps and the teacher's principled rationales, rather than merely mimicking outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) a novel distillation protocol for implanting self-supervised reasoning into small models, 2) a new benchmark for evaluating intrinsic reasoning quality in student models beyond task accuracy, and 3) open-source, efficient models capable of transparent CoT, advancing reproducible and pluralistic alignment testbeds.
proposals.novelty: While model distillation is common, applying a self-supervised, principle-based alignment objective—inspired by Quiet-STaR's core mechanism—to induce *latent* reasoning structures in small models is a novel and non-obvious direction beyond standard output imitation.

proposals.title: Inverse Cognitive Reconstruction: Inferring Latent Reasoning from Text via Geophysical Inversion Methods
proposals.motivation: Current methods for interpreting model outputs or inferring human thought processes are largely discriminative or rely on direct generation of rationales, lacking a principled framework for handling ambiguity and noise. Inverse problems in geophysics, like seismic tomography, provide a mature mathematical framework for reconstructing hidden structures from sparse, indirect measurements, which is analogous to inferring latent cognitive states from observed text.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing thought prediction as a regularized inverse problem, with a learned forward model mapping latent reasoning vectors to text, will yield more accurate, robust, and interpretable reconstructions of unstated rationales compared to end-to-end discriminative models.
proposals.method: We will first train a neural 'cognitive forward model' (a decoder-only transformer) on a synthetic dataset of (thought_vector, text_output) pairs, where thought vectors are structured sequences representing reasoning steps. For inversion, given a new text, we will optimize for the latent thought vector using gradient-based methods (e.g., L-BFGS) with geophysics-inspired regularizers like Tikhonov (smoothness over reasoning steps) and L1 (sparsity). The benchmark will be a novel inductive test set requiring recovery of implicit principles from generated text.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, model-based inversion framework for cognitive analysis, a reproducible benchmark for evaluating inverse reasoning reconstruction, and a method for generating interpretable, regularized 'cognitive tomograms' from language model outputs.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct adaptation of formal geophysical inversion techniques to cognitive modeling and in framing rationale inference as an iterative optimization problem with explicit physical analogies, moving beyond purely data-driven or prompted generation methods common in pre-2026 interpretability research.

proposals.title: Inverse Chain-of-Thought: Recovering Latent Reasoning Traces from Model-Generated Text
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) generate text, but the underlying reasoning process—the 'thought'—remains a latent, unobserved variable. This opacity hinders interpretability, trust, and the ability to refine reasoning. An inverse model that predicts the likely unstated rationale preceding a text output would provide a crucial window into model cognition, enabling new forms of analysis and oversight.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reasoning traces (e.g., chain-of-thought steps) that likely preceded a given text output can be accurately reconstructed by a model trained on a synthetically generated dataset of (rationale, output) pairs, using principles of mutual information maximization and self-supervised alignment.
proposals.method: We will create a large-scale, multi-task dataset by using strong LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, Claude) to generate diverse reasoning traces (thoughts) and their corresponding final text outputs across reasoning, writing, and code-generation tasks. We will then train a T5 or encoder-decoder transformer model using a contrastive, self-supervised objective inspired by Zelikman's 'Self-Supervised Alignment with Mutual Information' to maximize the mutual information between the output text and the inferred rationale, without requiring human preference labels. The model will be benchmarked on held-out synthetic data and a novel 'PERSONA'-style testbed for pluralistic evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel model and benchmark for the inverse reasoning task, 2) a self-supervised method for aligning text with latent rationales, and 3) a reproducible testbed, extending the PERSONA framework, to evaluate the plurality and uncertainty of inferred thoughts. This advances the field of interpretability and model introspection.
proposals.novelty: While prior work generates text from thoughts (chain-of-thought), the inverse problem of reliably inferring thought from text is largely unexplored. Our approach is novel in framing it as a self-supervised alignment task, avoiding the need for costly human rationale annotations.

proposals.title: Dual: Shaping Internal Reasoning Policies via Rationale Preference Modeling
proposals.motivation: Current methods for improving language model reasoning, such as chain-of-thought prompting or supervised fine-tuning on rationales, often rely on static, final-answer supervision. This fails to provide scalable, granular feedback on the quality of the reasoning process itself. Drawing a direct analogy from the success of RLHF in aligning model outputs, we posit that applying similar principles to shape the internal *generation* of reasoning traces could lead to more robust, efficient, and transparent problem-solving.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual-system framework, where a learned reward model trained on human preferences over reasoning steps is used to optimize a language model's internal rationale generation policy via reinforcement learning, will produce reasoning traces that are more coherent, correct, and data-efficient than those from supervised fine-tuning alone.
proposals.method: We will first construct a benchmark of tasks requiring multi-step reasoning (e.g., GSM8K, PrOntoQA) and collect human preference labels between pairs of generated rationales. Using this dataset, we will train a 'rationale reward model' (RRM), a transformer encoder that scores the quality of a reasoning trace. This RRM will then provide dense reward signals to guide the policy optimization of a primary language model using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). The policy model's actions will be the generation of individual reasoning tokens, with the RRM scoring intermediate and completed rationales, enabling iterative refinement of the thinking process.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel training paradigm for refining language model reasoning at the process level, 2) a reproducible methodology and testbed for rationale preference modeling and policy shaping, and 3) empirical evidence on the scalability and limits of RLHF-inspired techniques for internal cognitive processes in LLMs.
proposals.novelty: While RLHF is standard for optimizing final outputs, applying it to directly shape the internal, step-by-step reasoning policy—treating rationale generation as a reinforcement learning problem guided by a learned preference model over thoughts—is a novel and non-obvious extension of alignment principles to the domain of model cognition.

proposals.title: The Dynamics of Alignment: A Testbed for Measuring Temporal Drift and Strategic Adaptation in Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current LLM evaluation relies heavily on static benchmarks, which provide a single snapshot of performance. This fails to capture how model behavior may evolve over time due to continued learning, strategic adaptation to user inputs, or drift in internal representations. This gap is critical for safety and reliability, as a model that passes a static safety test may later develop vulnerabilities or learn to circumvent its constraints through dynamic interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that language models exhibit significant and measurable dynamic behavior—including temporal drift, strategic goal adaptation, and principle circumvention—that is systematically missed by static, one-off benchmark evaluations.
proposals.method: We will build a dynamic testbed, extending the PERSONA pluralistic alignment framework, to subject models to longitudinal interaction sequences. We will generate a principled, multi-turn interaction dataset using a pipeline inspired by the Media Content Atlas, where tasks and adversarial probes evolve based on model responses. Core evaluation will use a self-supervised mutual information metric (inspired by Zelikman's prior work) to quantify drift between a model's stated principles and its evolving outputs over time, moving beyond static accuracy scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel methodology and open-source testbed for dynamic model evaluation, 2) empirical evidence quantifying the limitations of static benchmarks, and 3) new metrics for tracking behavioral drift and strategic adaptation in aligned models, directly informing safety practices.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates static benchmarks or alignment testbeds, our approach uniquely focuses on *temporal dynamics* and *strategic adaptation* as first-class evaluation targets, using principled dataset generation and self-supervised metrics to measure change, not just final-state performance.

proposals.title: PRISM: Probabilistic Representation for Inductive Subset Modeling to Achieve Evaluation Completeness
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of language and reasoning models relies on large, static benchmarks, which are costly to run and may still miss critical failure modes. This creates a tension between evaluation thoroughness and practical efficiency. A principled method to select a minimal, representative subset of test scenarios that guarantees comprehensive understanding of model capabilities would revolutionize evaluation practices.
proposals.hypothesis: A strategically selected, information-theoretically dense subset of evaluation scenarios, derived from a generative model of the task space, can provide a complete characterization of model performance equivalent to exhaustive testing on the full benchmark.
proposals.method: We will develop an inductive pipeline that first uses a multimodal LLM (inspired by the Media Content Atlas) to generate a diverse, principle-grounded space of evaluation scenarios. We will then apply a self-supervised mutual information maximization technique (extending Self-Supervised Alignment with Mutual Information) to learn a compressed, probabilistic representation of this space. Finally, we will derive a minimal subset that maximizes coverage of this representation, validating completeness via rigorous testing on held-out scenarios using a reproducible testbed framework like PERSONA.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and practical tool for efficient, yet provably comprehensive, model evaluation. It will advance the science of benchmark design by moving from large, enumerative sets to principled, generative ones, significantly reducing the computational cost of reliable assessment.
proposals.novelty: While subset selection for efficiency is common, the claim that a small subset can guarantee *evaluation completeness* is non-obvious and challenges conventional wisdom. Our approach is novel in its integration of generative task modeling with information-theoretic subset selection specifically for the goal of comprehensive characterization.

proposals.title: HELM-Live: A Framework for Continuous, Inductive Benchmarking of Evolving Language Models
proposals.motivation: The Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM) benchmark provides a crucial, multidimensional assessment of model capabilities. However, the rapid pace of model releases and the emergence of new failure modes (e.g., in reasoning or multimodal tasks) render static benchmarks obsolete quickly. A living benchmark is needed to provide timely, rigorous, and reproducible comparisons that reflect the current state of the field, guiding both research and deployment decisions.
proposals.hypothesis: A continuous, automated benchmarking framework that inductively incorporates new models and scenarios will produce more accurate, timely, and actionable evaluations than static benchmarks, revealing evolving capability trends and vulnerabilities.
proposals.method: We will develop an automated pipeline to ingest new model APIs and open-source checkpoints, running them against a continuously updated suite of core and novel scenarios. Inspired by principles-based alignment methods, we will inductively generate new adversarial test scenarios by analyzing failure patterns of frontier models. The system will use a versioned, reproducible testbed architecture (extending concepts from PERSONA) to ensure fair comparisons over time, with results dynamically visualized in an interactive dashboard akin to a Media Content Atlas.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a maintained, authoritative evaluation platform, delivering longitudinal analysis of model evolution. It will establish methodological standards for continuous benchmarking and generate a public dataset of model performance trajectories, enabling new research on capability forecasting and evaluation design.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates static benchmarks or testbeds, HELM-Live introduces a principled, inductive framework for the continuous and automated expansion of evaluation dimensions, transforming benchmarking from a periodic snapshot into a living measurement instrument.

proposals.title: PRISM: A Principle-Inductive Scenario Mapping for Comprehensive Language Model Evaluation
proposals.motivation: Current LM benchmarks, like the 16 core scenarios from HELM, provide a structured but limited snapshot of model capabilities. The vast, unstructured space of real-world LM use cases remains poorly covered, creating a risk of overfitting to narrow evaluations and missing critical failure modes. A systematic method to taxonomize and incorporate this long-tail of scenarios is essential for robust and trustworthy model assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying self-supervised, inductive principles to a large corpus of diverse, generated task descriptions, we can construct a comprehensive scenario taxonomy that significantly expands evaluation coverage and reveals capability gaps not captured by existing benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will first generate a massive, diverse seed set of potential LM use-case descriptions using a strong, prompting-based LLM. Then, building on the Self-Supervised Alignment with Mutual Information (SAMI) framework, we will learn a set of latent 'scenario principles' in an unsupervised manner from this corpus. These principles will be used to cluster scenarios into a structured taxonomy. Finally, we will implement a testbed, inspired by the PERSONA architecture, to generate executable evaluation instances for high-coverage clusters, prioritizing those orthogonal to the 16 core scenarios.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1) a novel, data-driven taxonomy of LM application scenarios, 2) an open-source pipeline for generating principled, scalable evaluations, and 3) a new benchmark suite that demonstrably increases scenario coverage and stress-tests model capabilities in previously overlooked dimensions.
proposals.novelty: Unlike manual or heuristic taxonomy creation, our approach inductively derives scenario dimensions directly from generated data. It uniquely combines Zelikman's expertise in principle-based learning (SAMI) and reproducible testbeds (PERSONA) to automate the expansion of evaluation frontiers.

proposals.title: META-EVAL: A Meta-Benchmark for Diagnosing Bias and Robustness in LLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation Frameworks
proposals.motivation: The reliance on large language models (LLMs) as evaluators (LLM-as-a-Judge) has become a cornerstone for benchmarking AI systems, from reasoning to content generation. However, pre-2026, these evaluators themselves are largely unexamined; their judgments may be biased by prompt phrasing, latent cultural assumptions in their training data, or sensitivity to irrelevant stylistic variations. This creates a critical gap: if our primary tool for measuring progress is flawed, our entire empirical foundation is unstable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic meta-evaluation will reveal that current LLM-as-a-Judge frameworks exhibit significant, task-dependent biases (e.g., verbosity, sycophancy) and a lack of robustness to semantically invariant perturbations, limiting their reliability as general-purpose benchmarks.
proposals.method: We will construct META-EVAL, a dataset and test suite generated using principles from Zelikman's prior work on self-supervised alignment and pluralistic testbeds. The method involves: 1) Generating a diverse corpus of model outputs for established tasks (e.g., summarization, reasoning) using a suite of base models. 2) Systematically creating controlled perturbations (e.g., paraphrases, adding irrelevant but fluent text, injecting subtle factual errors) to create 'adversarial' evaluation pairs. 3) Running these through a battery of popular LLM-as-a-Judge frameworks (e.g., GPT-4, Claude as judges with varied prompts). 4) Applying inductive analysis and mutual information-based metrics to quantify judge consistency, bias, and sensitivity, treating the judge's principles as latent variables to be inferred.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, publicly available meta-benchmark for stress-testing LLM-based evaluators. It will produce concrete, quantified findings on failure modes and biases, enabling researchers to select more robust evaluation protocols. Furthermore, it will advance methodology for creating self-supervised diagnostic tools in AI evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates benchmarks for model capabilities, META-EVAL uniquely benchmarks the evaluation frameworks themselves. It leverages a principled, generative approach to create a controlled testbed for meta-evaluation, moving beyond anecdotal criticism to systematic, empirical diagnosis.

proposals.title: STREAMLINE: A Self-Supervised Framework for Continuous, Real-Time Evaluation of Deployed Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current LLM evaluation relies on static benchmarks, which fail to capture performance on dynamic, real-world data streams and lead to rapid obsolescence. Deployed models encounter novel inputs and emergent failures that are absent from curated test sets, creating a critical gap between lab-based assessment and operational reliability. A system for continuous evaluation is essential for maintaining model safety, robustness, and alignment in production environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A self-supervised evaluation framework, leveraging mutual information and inductive principles, can generate reliable, real-time performance signals from unlabeled live data streams without human preference labels. This continuous signal will correlate with and predict downstream task failures more effectively than periodic static benchmarking.
proposals.method: We will build a pipeline that ingests live text streams (e.g., from APIs or social media). Inspired by Zelikman's work on principle-based alignment, the core is a testbed that automatically generates evaluation probes by applying a set of inductive principles (e.g., consistency, factual grounding) to the stream. A mutual information objective, as in 'Self-Supervised Alignment with Mutual Information,' will train lightweight probe models to distinguish between model outputs that satisfy or violate these principles. The system will output a real-time dashboard of principle adherence scores and anomaly alerts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first open-source framework for continuous, label-free evaluation of LLMs on live data, moving beyond static benchmarks. It will contribute a new methodology for operational monitoring and a reproducible testbed (extending PERSONA) for studying model drift and failure modes in dynamic environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates static benchmarks or uses human preferences, this system autonomously generates and scores evaluations in real-time using self-supervised learning on principles, enabling truly continuous assessment without a fixed dataset.

ideas.title: Continuously update HELM with new models and scenarios
ideas.description: Maintain HELM as a living benchmark by adding new models, scenarios, and metrics over time.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Apply Quiet-STaR to larger language models
ideas.description: Scale the Quiet-STaR training method to significantly larger base language models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Quiet-STaR: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Think Before Speaking

ideas.title: Faithfulness-Constrained Reinforcement Learning for Robust Rationale Generation
ideas.description: This approach trains a model with RL to generate rationales that maximize a composite reward balancing external helpfulness and internal faithfulness. The faithfulness reward is computed by measuring the rationale's consistency under input variations and its causal influence on the model's output, preventing the generation of plausible but deceptive reasoning. The training framework treats faithfulness as a hard constraint or a strong penalty, ensuring the RL policy does not deviate from generating truthful representations of the model's inference. The result is a system that produces rationales which are both useful to the user and verifiably aligned with the model's actual decision-making process.

ideas.title: Utility-Driven Rationale Generation via Meta-Learned Verifier Improvement
ideas.description: This system trains a primary model to generate rationales by using a meta-learning objective that maximizes the performance improvement of an auxiliary verifier model. The verifier is trained to solve the core task using only the rationale from the primary model, not the original input. The primary model's training signal comes from the gradient of the verifier's task loss with respect to the rationale, forcing rationales to be genuinely informative and sufficient for task solution. This creates a direct, non-proxy optimization target where rationale quality is defined by its downstream utility, preventing the generation of superficially plausible but useless reasoning.

ideas.title: Develop robust evaluation schemes for benchmarks
ideas.description: Create new evaluation methodologies that are less sensitive to minor benchmark perturbations.
ideas.sourceTitle: When Benchmarks are Targets: Revealing the Sensitivity of Large Language Model Leaderboards

ideas.title: Learning to Filter Rationales via Self-Supervised Auxiliary Losses
ideas.description: This research direction proposes training a rationale filtering module not with handcrafted rules, but by supervising it with auxiliary losses that directly target the quality and downstream utility of the rationales it selects. The key innovation is that the selection mechanism itself is parameterized and trained end-to-end, using signals from these auxiliary losses to learn a dynamic, context-aware policy for identifying high-quality reasoning traces. This moves beyond post-hoc filtering or simple reinforcement learning from task outcomes, instead enabling the model to develop and refine an internal representation of rationale effectiveness that can generalize across tasks and improve during training.

ideas.title: Apply measurement error models from metrology
ideas.description: Model benchmark scores with error distributions to account for inherent evaluation noise.
ideas.sourceTitle: When Benchmarks are Targets: Revealing the Sensitivity of Large Language Model Leaderboards

ideas.title: Rationale Consistency via Bidirectional Latent Alignment
ideas.description: This approach trains a language model with a composite objective. The primary task uses a standard language modeling loss on the final text output, while an auxiliary model is trained to perform the inverse problem: reconstructing the rationale from the final text. The key synergy is that the main model's rationale generation is then also optimized via an auxiliary loss to maximize the reconstructability of its own rationales from its outputs. This creates a cycle of consistency, ensuring generated rationales are not just useful for the task but are semantically embedded in the final text, improving their faithfulness and quality without requiring additional human annotations.

ideas.title: Using Generated Rationales as a Diagnostic Probe for Model Calibration
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a model's generated rationales (its 'thinking') are useful only for improving its direct predictive accuracy on the next token. Drawing an analogy from medical diagnostics, where a biopsy is analyzed to guide a separate treatment, we propose using rationales as a diagnostic probe. The model's generated chain-of-thought for a set of prompts is systematically analyzed by secondary classifiers or metrics to detect systemic flaws—such as unfaithful reasoning or biased associations—that are not apparent in the final output alone. The diagnostic results from this 'rationale biopsy' are then used as a training signal or a control parameter for a distinct intervention, like constrained decoding or targeted adversarial fine-tuning, aimed at correcting the identified flaw in the model's fundamental generation process, thereby improving attributes like faithfulness or reducing bias in its future completions.

ideas.title: Scale down: STaR for data-efficient learning
ideas.description: Apply STaR in extremely low-data regimes, starting with zero or one rationale example.
ideas.sourceTitle: STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning

ideas.description: Build a system that continuously evaluates deployed models on live data streams.

ideas.title: Static benchmarks cannot capture dynamic model behavior
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that a static snapshot evaluation fully captures model capabilities and risks.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Extend to multimodal reasoning tasks
ideas.description: Generalize the tokenwise rationale generation approach to multimodal inputs like images and audio.
ideas.sourceTitle: Quiet-STaR: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Think Before Speaking

ideas.title: Automate human selection from LLM hypotheses
ideas.description: Replace human selection of hypotheses with an automated method to improve the pipeline's efficiency and scalability.

ideas.title: Challenge the need for a human-written seed

ideas.title: Dual: Make models robust to benchmark quirks
ideas.description: Instead of fixing benchmarks, train or prompt models to be invariant to irrelevant perturbations.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: predict thought from text
ideas.description: Train a model to infer the likely unstated rationale that preceded a given piece of text.
ideas.sourceTitle: Quiet-STaR: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Think Before Speaking

ideas.title: Cross-Modal Attention Alignment for Tokenwise Multimodal Rationales
ideas.description: This idea generalizes tokenwise rationale generation to multimodal inputs by drawing an analogy from Geographic Information Systems (GIS). In GIS, disparate data types are fused by registering them to a shared spatial coordinate system. Similarly, we propose a novel neural architecture that projects inputs from text, image, and audio modalities into a unified 'reasoning space' via cross-modal attention layers. The alignment and interaction patterns within this space—specifically, the computed attention maps between modalities—provide the explicit, fine-grained rationales. For example, the model would generate not just a final answer but a map showing which image patches attended most strongly to which words in a question, thereby creating interpretable, token/patch-level explanations for multimodal reasoning tasks.

ideas.title: Improve hypothesis generation diversity and quality
ideas.description: Develop better prompting or fine-tuning strategies for LLMs to generate more diverse and correct abstract hypotheses.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hypothesis Search: Inductive Reasoning with Language Models

ideas.title: STaR with Self-Verified Knowledge Integration
ideas.description: This system enhances STaR by integrating external knowledge retrieval with a dedicated verification module. After a candidate rationale is generated using retrieved documents, the model must produce a self-critique that identifies potential contradictions, uncertainties, or irrelevancies in the used knowledge. Only rationales that pass a confidence threshold in this verification step are used for subsequent training or to produce a final answer, creating a more robust chain of reasoning that is resilient to noisy retrievals.

ideas.title: Training uses standard language modeling loss
ideas.description: Introduce auxiliary losses specifically for the quality or usefulness of the rationales.
ideas.sourceTitle: Quiet-STaR: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Think Before Speaking

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Generate questions from rationales
ideas.description: Use the learned reasoning capability to generate novel questions and their reasoning chains.

ideas.title: Adopt ensemble methods from machine learning
ideas.description: Use ensemble techniques over multiple benchmark perturbations to produce stable rankings.
ideas.sourceTitle: When Benchmarks are Targets: Revealing the Sensitivity of Large Language Model Leaderboards

ideas.title: Evaluate the evaluators: meta-benchmarking

ideas.title: Invert the focus from evaluation to generation
ideas.description: Build context-aware generation models first, then see if current metrics suffice for evaluation.

ideas.title: Scale down to few-shot hypothesis generation
ideas.description: Optimize the pipeline to work with very few candidate hypotheses under extreme computational or time constraints.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hypothesis Search: Inductive Reasoning with Language Models

ideas.title: Audited Rationale Generation for Self-Correction
ideas.description: This system generates rationales and then subjects them to evaluation by a separately trained 'Rationale Auditor' model, which is specialized in detecting unfaithfulness and bias. The auditor's feedback is converted into a reward for reinforcement learning, guiding the main model to produce better rationales. This creates a self-correcting loop where rationale quality is improved against an external standard, not just the model's own preferences. The approach directly targets the unreliability of using raw model outputs as their own training signal.

ideas.title: Apply STaR to more complex reasoning tasks
ideas.description: Extend the STaR method to more challenging domains like scientific reasoning or advanced mathematics.
ideas.sourceTitle: STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning

ideas.title: Inverse Cognitive Reconstruction via Geophysical Inversion Methods
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to inverse problems from geophysics—where subsurface properties are inferred from surface data—to the cognitive domain. The analogous problem is seismic tomography, where a forward model of wave propagation is combined with regularized inversion to reconstruct hidden structures. Here, we propose training a neural 'cognitive forward model' that maps a latent representation of a thought process (the unstated rationale) to a piece of text. Then, given a text output, we solve the inverse problem via iterative optimization with appropriate regularization (e.g., sparsity, smoothness over a reasoning chain, or adherence to a prior over plausible cognitive states) to infer the most likely preceding thought vector. This approach frames thought prediction as a structured, model-based inversion rather than a purely discriminative task.

ideas.title: Evaluation completeness is achievable with a subset
ideas.description: Challenge that a representative subset of scenarios guarantees comprehensive understanding.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Reliance on final answer correctness
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that only rationales leading to correct final answers are useful for training.
ideas.sourceTitle: STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning

ideas.title: Improve rationale filtering and selection
ideas.description: Develop better methods for identifying and selecting high-quality rationales from the generated pool.
ideas.sourceTitle: STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning

ideas.title: Scale task complexity, not just model size
ideas.description: Systematically increase reasoning depth and required steps in tasks during STaR iterations.
ideas.sourceTitle: STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning

ideas.title: Compositional Concept-Vector Rationales with Guaranteed Decodability
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond the strict dichotomy of natural language or opaque latent vectors by designing rationales as compositions of human-interpretable concept tokens and their latent relational vectors. The system uses a vector-quantized bottleneck to learn a discrete codebook of concepts, ensuring every latent step can be mapped back to a symbolic primitive. During reasoning, the model manipulates sparse combinations of these concept vectors, and a lightweight, deterministic decoder guarantees that any internal state can be translated into a coherent natural language phrase for full auditability, combining the efficiency of latent space reasoning with the transparency of symbolic AI.

ideas.title: Dual: Feedback-Guided Rationale Refinement via Policy Shaping
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept of using external feedback to train thinking by drawing a direct analogy from Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). The analogous solution involves training a proxy reward model on human preferences and using it to guide policy optimization. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to implement a 'Dual' training system where a 'rationale reward model' is trained on human or algorithmic feedback that compares the quality of generated reasoning traces (e.g., for coherence, correctness, efficiency). This reward model is then integrated into the training loop of the primary model, not to score final answers, but to provide dense reinforcement learning signals that directly shape the model's internal reasoning policy. This enables scalable, iterative refinement of the thinking process itself, guided by external feedback.

ideas.title: Increase scenario coverage beyond the 16 core

ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Create a dual benchmark for value explicitness

ideas.title: Scale up: Multi-iteration STaR on huge datasets

ideas.description: Generate hypotheses directly in a formal or structured representation, bypassing the natural language abstraction step.

ideas.title: Extend hypothesis search to more complex domains
ideas.description: Apply the multi-level hypothesis generation and testing framework to reasoning tasks beyond ARC, SyGuS, and List Functions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hypothesis Search: Inductive Reasoning with Language Models

ideas.title: Dual: Generate problems from a hypothesis
ideas.description: Given a high-level hypothesis, automatically generate novel reasoning problems that satisfy it.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hypothesis Search: Inductive Reasoning with Language Models

ideas.title: Sparse Latent Rationale Routing
ideas.description: This research direction explores using sparse activation patterns to dynamically route information through a set of specialized, non-linguistic latent modules for internal reasoning. Instead of generating verbose natural language tokens for each reasoning step, the model learns to compose sparse mixtures of these latent 'expert' representations, each capturing a distinct reasoning primitive (e.g., comparison, deduction, abstraction). This achieves the efficiency of sparse compute while operating on a compressed, high-bandwidth latent space, making the reasoning process more interpretable than a dense monolithic network but far more computationally efficient than generating full natural language rationales.

ideas.title: Architecturally-Grounded Dynamic Computation Segments for Rationale Generation
ideas.description: This approach structures rationale generation around the model's native computational architecture, defining reasoning segments that align with transformer layer groups or other structural modules. A lightweight, trainable halting network dynamically decides when to finalize a segment and emit a rationale, allowing variable-length 'thoughts' that are semantically coherent and traceable to specific internal state transitions. This ensures the generated rationales are faithful to the model's actual reasoning pathway, eliminating the arbitrariness of token-agnostic blocks while preserving flexible, non-sequential explanation.

ideas.title: Assume a single score suffices for ranking
ideas.description: Invert the assumption that a single aggregate benchmark score is sufficient for robust model comparison.
ideas.sourceTitle: When Benchmarks are Targets: Revealing the Sensitivity of Large Language Model Leaderboards

ideas.title: Scale up reasoning horizon beyond tokens
ideas.description: Extend the rationale generation to plan over longer sequences like paragraphs or documents.
ideas.sourceTitle: Quiet-STaR: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Think Before Speaking

ideas.title: Use metric learning for manifold distances
ideas.description: Incorporate metric learning techniques to improve the distance measure between points on conditional submanifolds.
ideas.sourceTitle: Evaluating the Disentanglement of Deep Generative Models through Manifold Topology

ideas.title: Formalize and test hybrid scoring methods
ideas.description: Systematically validate the proposed hybrid answer selection method across diverse models and tasks.
ideas.sourceTitle: When Benchmarks are Targets: Revealing the Sensitivity of Large Language Model Leaderboards

ideas.title: Scale hypothesis search to massive problem sets
ideas.description: Develop distributed systems to run the hypothesis generation and testing pipeline on thousands of reasoning problems concurrently.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hypothesis Search: Inductive Reasoning with Language Models

ideas.title: Apply STaR with Hierarchical Chain-of-Thought for Complex Scientific Reasoning
ideas.description: This research idea extends the STaR method to more challenging domains like scientific reasoning or advanced mathematics by drawing an analogy from computational chemistry's approach to protein folding. The analogous problem—navigating a vast, complex search space—is solved hierarchically, as seen in tools like Rosetta, which breaks folding into predictable secondary structure, tertiary assembly, and atomic refinement. The adapted solution applies this hierarchical, coarse-to-fine decomposition to the STaR framework. For a complex problem, the model is first prompted to generate a high-level solution blueprint or strategy. STaR's rationalization and verification process is then applied recursively to each sub-step, generating and verifying the intermediate reasoning chains. Finally, the verified sub-reasoning is synthesized into a complete, validated solution. This hierarchical STaR approach makes the iterative self-teaching loop tractable for problems where a single, end-to-end reasoning chain is too long or complex to generate and verify reliably.

ideas.title: Study the inverse: from programs to hypotheses
ideas.description: Investigate the reverse process of explaining given correct programs by inferring the underlying abstract natural language hypotheses.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hypothesis Search: Inductive Reasoning with Language Models

ideas.title: Thinking only helps predict future tokens
ideas.description: Use generated rationales for other objectives like improving faithfulness or reducing bias.
ideas.sourceTitle: Quiet-STaR: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Think Before Speaking

ideas.title: Assume referenceless metrics are inherently flawed
ideas.description: Investigate if referenceless metrics can ever be fully adequate, or if reference-based approaches are essential.
ideas.sourceTitle: Context Matters for Image Descriptions for Accessibility: Challenges for Referenceless Evaluation Metrics

ideas.title: Assume the LM cannot generate novel improvement strategies
ideas.description: Constrain the LM to only use a fixed, known set of optimization algorithms for self-improvement.
ideas.sourceTitle: Self-Taught Optimizer (STOP): Recursively Self-Improving Code Generation

ideas.title: Applying fMRI Decoding Techniques to Thought Token Semantics
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methodologies from cognitive neuroscience, specifically fMRI-based neural decoding, to investigate learned thought token semantics in language models. In neuroimaging, scientists successfully decode the semantic content of human thoughts from patterns of brain activity using techniques like multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and encoding models. By directly analogizing the model's internal activation patterns (e.g., from a specialized reasoning or 'chain-of-thought' layer) to neural activity patterns, we can apply similar decoding approaches. The adapted idea involves collecting activation data from the model's thought tokens generated during a diverse set of reasoning tasks, then training probe models (like linear classifiers or small neural networks) to map these activation vectors to interpretable labels—such as semantic categories, logical relations (e.g., 'implies', 'contradicts'), or intermediate reasoning steps. This would systematically reveal what semantic features, concepts, or operational primitives are consistently and reliably represented within the model's internal thought tokens, providing a mechanistic understanding of its learned reasoning representations.

ideas.title: STaR with Dynamic, Context-Aware Retrieval Augmentation
ideas.description: This idea proposes enhancing the STaR (Self-Taught Reasoner) method by integrating retrieval of external knowledge sources during rationale generation, inspired by solutions in computational biology. The analogous field is Protein Structure Prediction, specifically AlphaFold2's solution, which dynamically integrates external data like multiple sequence alignments and structural templates. Its Evoformer module decides which external information to prioritize based on the specific protein sequence's context. Adapted to STaR, this means the language model would not just retrieve facts but would learn to generate context-sensitive retrieval queries during its chain-of-thought. It would dynamically decide when to seek external knowledge, which tools or databases to consult, and how to integrate this information to refine its subsequent reasoning steps iteratively, making the retrieval process an active, learned component of the rationale generation loop.

ideas.title: Use visual question answering for context probing
ideas.description: Employ VQA models to generate context-specific questions and check description answers.
ideas.sourceTitle: Context Matters for Image Descriptions for Accessibility: Challenges for Referenceless Evaluation Metrics

ideas.title: Parallelize the search across many LLMs
ideas.description: Distribute the generation and testing of function implementations across a large cluster of LLM instances.
ideas.sourceTitle: Parsel🦆: Algorithmic Reasoning with Language Models by Composing Decompositions

ideas.title: Rationale generation requires answer-first prompting
ideas.description: Invert the STaR loop by first generating a rationale, then deriving an answer from it.
ideas.sourceTitle: STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning

ideas.title: Optimize the search over program implementations
ideas.description: Create more efficient search or pruning techniques for the space of Python programs generated from natural language hypotheses.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hypothesis Search: Inductive Reasoning with Language Models

ideas.title: Hierarchical Chunking with Semantic Landmarks for Document-Scale Reasoning
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from cartography and GIS to scale reasoning beyond tokens. The core problem—navigating and planning over vast information spaces—is analogous to map-making for large geographic regions. The analogous solution uses hierarchical chunking and salient landmarks to enable reasoning at multiple scales. Adapted to NLP, the idea is to develop a model that first processes a long document (paragraph or multi-document) to build a hierarchical semantic representation. This involves identifying high-level 'landmarks' such as core arguments, narrative turns, or key entities. The reasoning process then uses this structure not to process every token sequentially, but to plan a path through these landmarks, zooming in to token-level detail only when necessary for specific inferences, thereby extending the effective reasoning horizon to the document scale.

ideas.title: Externally-Grounded Rationale Verification via Actionable Execution
ideas.description: This research develops methods to move beyond textual filtering by grounding rationale evaluation in executable verification. The system parses generated reasoning chains into actionable sub-steps that can be executed by external tools, such as running a code snippet to check a calculation, querying a curated fact database, or simulating a logical outcome. The fidelity and results of these executions provide objective, external signals for selecting rationales. This approach directly addresses the insularity of prior methods by tethering rationale quality to verifiable performance in a constrained environment, promoting the selection of reasoning that is not just plausible but demonstrably correct and actionable.

ideas.title: Semantic Steering of Internal Rationales for Controllable Generation
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the learned semantics of a model's internal 'thought' tokens to identify subspaces or features corresponding to specific properties like factual grounding, social bias, or logical coherence. By mapping these semantic representations, we can then steer or constrain the model's internal rationales during generation to explicitly optimize for secondary objectives such as increased faithfulness or decreased toxicity. This creates a closed-loop system where the analysis of thought semantics directly informs interventions in the reasoning process, moving beyond using rationales merely as a byproduct for predicting the next token. The result is a methodology for controllable, transparent, and property-specific refinement of a language model's internal reasoning.

ideas.title: Apply noisy student training paradigm
ideas.description: Use a larger 'teacher' model to generate rationales for training a smaller 'student' model iteratively.
ideas.sourceTitle: STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning

ideas.title: Formalize and analyze discovered self-improvement strategies
ideas.description: Systematically categorize and benchmark the strategies (e.g., beam search) proposed by the LM.
ideas.sourceTitle: Self-Taught Optimizer (STOP): Recursively Self-Improving Code Generation

ideas.title: Scale down evaluation for rapid model development
ideas.description: Create a lightweight, fast version of HELM for iterative development cycles.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Joint Optimization of Sampling Efficiency and Quality for Language Generation
ideas.description: This research develops a framework for parallel sampling algorithms that explicitly models and optimizes the trade-off between computational efficiency and sample quality. Instead of treating speed as the sole objective, the system uses a multi-objective loss function that incorporates both latency metrics and learned or human-evaluated measures of textual diversity, coherence, and creativity. The approach dynamically adjusts sampling strategies based on the target application's tolerance for speed versus quality. This results in a family of adaptable sampling methods that are not just faster, but are provably Pareto-optimal within a defined quality-efficiency frontier for practical deployment.

ideas.title: Scale down to minimal compute and model size
ideas.description: Implement STOP with very small LMs or limited query budgets.
ideas.sourceTitle: Self-Taught Optimizer (STOP): Recursively Self-Improving Code Generation

ideas.title: Use Monte Carlo Tree Search for planning
ideas.description: Apply MCTS from game AI to guide the search over possible function implementation combinations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Parsel🦆: Algorithmic Reasoning with Language Models by Composing Decompositions

ideas.title: Adaptive Ensemble Filtering for Rationale Selection Using a Multi-Criteria Fitness Landscape
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from evolutionary biology to the problem of filtering rationales. In population genetics, natural selection operates on a multi-dimensional fitness landscape to filter genetic variants. Analogously, we propose to develop an adaptive ensemble filtering method for rationale selection. Each generated rationale is evaluated as an 'individual' with a fitness score defined by a landscape of multiple criteria (e.g., logical consistency, factual grounding, novelty, simplicity). The core innovation is an algorithm that models the non-linear interactions and trade-offs between these criteria, iteratively selecting a subset of rationales that represent a high-fitness, diverse 'population' optimal for downstream tasks, moving beyond simple weighted-average scoring methods.

ideas.title: Benchmark instruction-tuned and RLHF models systematically
ideas.description: Apply the holistic framework to systematically compare instruction-tuned and RLHF models against base LMs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Solve the inverse problem: specification inference
ideas.description: Given a working program, use Parsel-like reasoning to infer the high-level natural language specification.
ideas.sourceTitle: Parsel🦆: Algorithmic Reasoning with Language Models by Composing Decompositions

ideas.title: Sparse Activation Routing with Self-Correcting Verification Pathways
ideas.description: This approach implements efficient thinking via sparse expert mixtures but adds a parallel, low-cost verification network that monitors the quality of the routing decisions. When the verification signal indicates potential error propagation or uncertainty, the system triggers a localized, dense recomputation to correct the activation pathway for the affected modules. This creates a robust, self-correcting mechanism that maintains the efficiency of sparse computation while preventing catastrophic failure from poor routing. The result is a system that is both computationally efficient and reliable for complex, sequential reasoning tasks.

ideas.title: Evaluate human-LM collaboration, not just LM
ideas.description: Shift evaluation from the LM alone to the performance of a human using the LM.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Sparse Neural Activation via Immune System-Inspired Clonal Selection
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the efficiency problem in adaptive immune systems—clonal selection—to the context of sparse neural network activation. In immunology, the system does not activate all lymphocytes for every pathogen; instead, it uses antigen-receptor affinity matching to select and clone only the most relevant cells for a targeted, resource-efficient response. We propose implementing an analogous 'neural clonal selection' mechanism within a model. For each input batch, a fast, lightweight controller network would compute an affinity score between the input and many small, specialized sub-networks (or parameter blocks). Only the top-k highest-affinity sub-networks are then fully activated and tasked with processing that input, with their outputs combined. This creates a sparse, dynamic, and input-adaptive activation pattern, transferring the immune system's efficiency solution to reduce computational cost in machine learning models.

ideas.title: Improve test generation and validation strategies
ideas.description: Create more sophisticated methods for automatic test case generation and function validation within the Parsel search.
ideas.sourceTitle: Parsel🦆: Algorithmic Reasoning with Language Models by Composing Decompositions

ideas.title: Combine STaR with external knowledge sources
ideas.description: Enhance STaR by integrating retrieval of external facts or tools during rationale generation.
ideas.sourceTitle: STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning

ideas.title: Self-Critical Rationale Refinement with Feedback on Meta-Cognition
ideas.description: This approach trains a model to generate an initial rationale, then produce a self-critique identifying potential errors or gaps. It refines the rationale based on this critique. External feedback (human or algorithmic) is applied primarily to the quality of the self-critique and the improvement demonstrated in the refinement step, rather than to the initial rationale. This incentivizes the model to develop robust internal reasoning checks and a deeper understanding of what constitutes a valid reasoning step, making the thinking process more robust to superficial reward hacking.

ideas.title: Use epidemiological models for disinformation spread
ideas.description: Model the potential spread of LM-generated disinformation using contagion models.
ideas.sourceTitle: Holistic Evaluation of Language Models

ideas.title: Scale up to dynamic or video content
ideas.description: Extend context-aware evaluation to sequential visual media with temporal context.
ideas.sourceTitle: Context Matters for Image Descriptions for Accessibility: Challenges for Referenceless Evaluation Metrics

ideas.title: Relax the need for verifiable programs
ideas.description: Develop methods to reason with hypotheses that are probabilistic or cannot be perfectly verified by execution.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hypothesis Search: Inductive Reasoning with Language Models

ideas.title: Iterative Refinement via Computational Fluid Dynamics Solvers
ideas.description: This idea adapts the iterative solver methodology from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) to enable iterative refinement of a single thought in a cognitive architecture. In CFD, solvers like PISO iteratively predict a flow field, calculate a pressure residual, and apply a correction until convergence. Analogously, for thought generation, the system would initialize a 'thought state', then enter a loop: 1) Evaluate the current thought against a target (e.g., logical consistency, factual grounding) to produce a 'residual' metric. 2) Apply a targeted, computationally cheaper 'correction operator' (akin to a pressure-correction equation) to update the thought state based on this residual. 3) Repeat for a fixed number of cycles or until the residual falls below a threshold. This creates a multi-cycle refinement process for a single reasoning trace, improving quality through successive approximation rather than one-shot generation or simple sampling.

ideas.title: Use Monte Carlo Tree Search for exploration
ideas.description: Guide the hypothesis search space exploration using planning algorithms like Monte Carlo Tree Search.
ideas.sourceTitle: Hypothesis Search: Inductive Reasoning with Language Models

ideas.title: Static few-shot rationale examples
ideas.description: Challenge using fixed few-shot examples; dynamically select or generate exemplars per question.
ideas.sourceTitle: STaR: Bootstrapping Reasoning With Reasoning

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Evaluate context from description
ideas.description: Create a metric that evaluates how well a description explains the surrounding context.
ideas.sourceTitle: Context Matters for Image Descriptions for Accessibility: Challenges for Referenceless Evaluation Metrics

ideas.title: Scale Parsel to massive problem decomposition
ideas.description: Apply Parsel to decompose and implement extremely large software systems with thousands of functions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Parsel🦆: Algorithmic Reasoning with Language Models by Composing Decompositions

ideas.title: Cognitive Scaffolding: Using a Chain-of-Thought Curriculum to Distill Reasoning into Small Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts the educational concept of 'scaffolding' and 'cognitive apprenticeship' from developmental psychology to distill reasoning capabilities into small student models. The analogous solution involves an expert (teacher model) externalizing its internal chain-of-thought reasoning for a set of problems, creating a structured curriculum where complexity is gradually increased. The adapted method trains the small model in stages: it first learns to complete the final, simplest inferences of the teacher's reasoning chains, then works backward to predict earlier, more abstract reasoning steps, with the teacher's supervisory signal progressively reduced. This process aims to instill robust, generalizable reasoning patterns in the small model, mirroring how a tutor guides a student to internalize complex problem-solving skills.

ideas.title: Adaptive Block Sampling via Monte Carlo Markov Chain Partitioning
ideas.description: This idea adapts the domain decomposition strategy from parallel molecular dynamics to optimize parallel sampling for language models. The core problem in both fields is the high cost of synchronizing parallel processes after every minimal computational step (a timestep for atoms, a token for LLMs). The analogous solution is to partition the domain—spatial for molecules, sequential for tokens—into blocks. Adapted to sampling, this means developing an algorithm where a draft model proposes a block of future tokens. The sequence is then divided into sub-blocks, and multiple parallel verifier samplers evaluate and correct these sub-blocks against the target model independently, only synchronizing their results at the end of the block. This adaptive block sampling approach aims to maximize computational efficiency by minimizing inter-processor communication, making parallel sampling viable for longer sequences where tokenwise synchronization is a bottleneck.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Multi-Granularity Planning for Coherent Long-Form Rationale Generation
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the intractability of long-horizon reasoning by structuring the planning process hierarchically. The system first produces a coarse, abstract plan capturing the global document structure and argument flow. This top-down constraint guides subsequent, more detailed planning at the paragraph and sentence level, ensuring local decisions contribute to a globally coherent rationale. This method enables tractable, deep reasoning over entire documents by breaking the problem into manageable, interdependent sub-problems.

ideas.title: Evolutionary Shaping of Rationales via Multi-Objective Reward Landscapes
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology's optimal foraging theory, where behaviors evolve to maximize energy gain while minimizing costs and risks in a complex fitness landscape, we adapt this to rationale generation. The analogous solution involves shaping behavior through multi-objective selective pressures rather than a single goal. The adapted research idea applies RL to train a model to generate rationales that maximize a composite reward function. This function balances several competing objectives: the primary utility (e.g., correctness on a downstream task), a cost for rationale length/complexity, and a penalty for internal contradictions or detectable flaws, thereby evolving rationales that are optimally efficient and robust, not just maximally verbose.

ideas.title: Guarded Iterative Refinement with Meta-Cognitive Validity Monitoring
ideas.description: This system integrates iterative thought refinement with a parallel, lightweight monitoring module that performs validity checks on each reasoning cycle. The monitor assesses coherence, relevance to the original prompt, and logical progression, using learned heuristics to identify degenerative loops or context drift. If a cycle fails these checks, the system can either reset to a prior robust state or pivot to a substantively different reasoning strategy, preventing collapse. This ensures iterative refinement is a controlled, self-correcting process that enhances rather than degrades reasoning quality.

ideas.title: Apply Formal Verification Synthesis from Hardware Design to Structured Reasoning
ideas.description: This idea adapts the formal verification synthesis methodology from hardware design to structured reasoning tasks. In hardware design, engineers specify system properties using formal languages (e.g., PSL, SVA), and automated tools synthesize verification code or assertions to prove a design meets its specification through executable checks. Similarly, for structured reasoning, we propose generating rationales not as natural language or simple code, but as formal statements in a specification logic. A program synthesis engine would then take these specifications and automatically generate executable verification code or formal proof objects for each reasoning step. This ensures every inferential step in the rationale is machine-verifiable, logically sound, and traceable, directly parallel to how hardware designs are rigorously verified against their formal specifications before implementation.

ideas.title: Causal Ablation of Internal Thought Semantics to Isolate Functionally Necessary Representations
ideas.description: This research moves beyond descriptive analysis to causally test the functional role of semantics within a model's generated internal thoughts. It designs a framework that performs targeted interventions on the thought token stream during multi-step reasoning tasks, systematically ablating or altering hypothesized semantic features (e.g., sub-goal specification, variable binding, rule recall). The causal impact of each intervention on task performance and reasoning trajectory is measured to distinguish necessary computational elements from incidental correlates. This yields a mechanistic understanding of how thought tokens contribute to the reasoning process.

ideas.title: Variable-Length Neural Computation Blocks via Biological Oscillator Synchronization
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that language model reasoning must be strictly synchronized with token generation. Drawing from computational neuroscience's understanding of neural oscillations, where biological information processing occurs in variable-duration windows synchronized by theta/gamma rhythms rather than discrete neuron firings, we propose adapting this to transformer architectures. The analogous solution involves neural assemblies forming transient functional units whose activation periods are determined by phase-coupling rather than fixed time steps. Adapted to language models, this would create a transformer variant where attention computations occur in variable-duration 'thought cycles' rather than per-token steps, implementing a phase-synchronization mechanism that dynamically groups tokens into processing blocks of variable size. Computation would continue within each block until an internal 'phase reset' signal indicates completion of a coherent thought unit, allowing rationale generation to proceed independently of token boundaries while maintaining synchronization where semantically appropriate.
